chunks.text: Deniz Jafari Research Statement My research focuses on advancing human-machine interaction through the integration of wearable sensing, immersive technologies, and robotic systems to augment human capability, support rehabilitation, and improve assistive care. I am particularly interested in symbiotic systems in which technology can sense human movement, interpret performance and user needs, and provide adaptive support through feedback, gamification, or physical assistance. A central theme of my work is the use of wearable sensors to understand and optimize human performance. During my PhD, I use inertial measurement units and headset-based sensing, including the Meta Quest, to track upper-limb and trunk movement during post-stroke rehabilitation exercises. These systems enable real-time assessment of movement quality, exercise performance, and compensatory strategies that may limit recovery. By capturing these data outside conventional laboratory or clinical settings, my work aims to support more personalized and accessible rehabilitation. My broader research vision extends beyond rehabilitation monitoring toward integrated humancentered systems that combine sensing, computation, and hardware. Wearable sensors provide a foundation for understanding movement quality, user preferences, engagement, and functional performance. These data can then be used to optimize therapeutic tasks, personalize feedback, and guide the design of assistive or augmentative technologies, including prosthetic systems, exoskeletons, and rehabilitation robots.

I am especially interested in how robotic components can be used not only to restore lost function, but also to enhance human capability through safe, intuitive, and adaptive physical interaction. A major component of my research involves validating wearable sensing approaches against goldstandard motion capture methods to establish their accuracy, reliability, and clinical relevance. By combining biomechanical modelling, sensor-to-segment calibration, real-time movement analysis, and designing immersive game-based environments, I aim to develop systems that make repetitive rehabilitation exercises more engaging while providing objective measures of performance and progress. Ultimately, my research seeks to bridge biomechanics, wearable sensing, robotics, and humancomputer interaction to create intelligent technologies that adapt to the user. My goal is to contribute to future systems that can sense, learn from, and physically interact with humans to improve rehabilitation outcomes, support independence, and expand human capability.

Research Article 3D Video Tracking Technology in the Assessment of Orofacial Impairments in Neurological Disease: Clinical Validation Deniz Jafari,a,b Leif Simmatis,a,b Diego Guarin,c Liziane Bouvier,a,d Babak Taati,b and Yana Yunusovaa,b,d a Department of Speech-Language Pathology, Rehabilitation Sciences Institute, University of Toronto, Ontario, Canada b KITE, Toronto Rehabilitation Institute, University Health Network, Ontario, Canada c University of Florida, Gainesville d Hurvitz Brain Sciences Program, Sunnybrook Research Institute, Toronto, Ontario, Canada A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Purpose: This study sought to determine whether clinically interpretable kineArticle History: matic features extracted automatically from three-dimensional (3D) videos were Received June 1, 2022 correlated with corresponding perceptual clinical orofacial ratings in individuals Revision received September 9, 2022 with orofacial impairments due to neurological disorders. Accepted January 10, 2023 Method: 45 participants (19 diagnosed with motor neuron diseases [MNDs] and 26 poststroke) performed two nonspeech tasks (mouth opening and lip spreadEditor-in-Chief: Mili Kuruvilla-Dugdale ing) and one speech task (repetition of a sentence "Buy Bobby a Puppy") while Editor: Jun Wang being video-recorded in a standardized lab setting. The color video recordings of participants were assessed by an expert clinician-a speech language https://doi.org/10.1044/2023_JSLHR-22-00321 pathologist-on the severity of three orofacial measures: symmetry, range of motion (ROM), and speed.

Clinically interpretable 3D kinematic features, linked to symmetry, ROM, and speed, were automatically extracted from video recordings, using a deep facial landmark detection and tracking algorithm for each of the three tasks. Spearman correlations were used to identify features that were significantly correlated (p value < .05) with their corresponding clinical scores. Clinically significant kinematic features were then used in the subsequent multivariate regression models to predict the overall orofacial impairment severity score. Results: Several kinematic features extracted from 3D video recordings were associated with their corresponding perceptual clinical scores, indicating clinical validity of these automatically derived measures. Different patterns of significant features were observed between MND and poststroke groups; these differences were aligned with clinical expectations in both cases. Conclusions: The results show that kinematic features extracted automatically from simple clinical tasks can capture characteristics used by clinicians during assessments. These findings support the clinical validity of video-based automatic extraction of kinematic features. Neurological disorders often affect orofacial and (i.e., inability to produce facial expressions or perform speech functions (Jani & Gore, 2014). For example, peo- volitional orofacial movements), as well as dysarthria, a ple poststroke (PS) or living with motor neuron diseases motor speech disorder (Flowers et al., 2013; Makkonen (MNDs) often experience orofacial motor impairments et al., 2016).

Accurate assessment of orofacial speech and nonspeech movements becomes important for effective disease detection, tracking progression, and monitoring treatment outcomes (Allison et al., 2017). OPEN = five repetitions of maximum jaw opening and closing; SPREAD = five repetitions of pretending to smile with tight lips; BBP = "Buy Bobby a Puppy." In this exploratory study, we investigated clinical validity of a 3D camera and vision-based facial tracking algorithm as an objective assessment tool for patients with neurological disorders-MND and PS. A relatively small, clinically interpretable lip and jaw kinematic feature set was delineated from the outset; the features were chosen to conceptually represent the measures used by clinicians to assess orofacial deficits during the oromotor examinations of nonspeech and speech tasks. The study revealed that subsets of kinematic features were significantly correlated with their corresponding clinical scores for each clinical group.

The sets of statistically significant features that were able to explain a significant portion of data variability in the overall severity scores in most models were unique for each of the clinical subgroups. Overall, the findings demonstrated the potential of automated videobased systems for extraction of kinematic features in the Deniz et al.: Clinical Validation of Orofacial Assessment Tool 3157

3D VIDEO TRACKING TECHNOLOGY IN THE ASSESSMENT OF OROFACIAL IMPAIRMENTS IN NEUROLOGICAL DISORDERS By Deniz Jafari A thesis submitted in conformity with the requirements for the degree of Master of Science Rehabilitation Sciences Institute University of Toronto © Copyright 2022 by Deniz Jafari

DERS By Deniz Jafari A thesis submitted in conformity with the requirements for the degree of Master of Science Rehabilitation Sciences Institute University of Toronto © Copyright 2022 by Deniz Jafari

3D video tracking technology in the assessment of orofacial impairments in neurological Disorders Deniz Jafari Master of Science Rehabilitation Sciences Institute University of Toronto Abstract & Keywords Changes to the orofacial muscles' movement and speech are often among the earliest signs perceived in Neurological disorders. Detection of subtle changes in speech and facial movements can help with the diagnosis and prognosis of neurological disorders. Deep artificial intelligent video-based facial analysis models have the potential to be used as objective and non-invasive clinical tools. This thesis used the V3 framework for evaluation of digital biomarkers and their adoption into clinical settings to evaluate an automatic video-based facial analysis system as an objective assessment tool for accessing orofacial movements. The proposed system consists of a 3D camera and Artificial Intelligent-based algorithms that automatically extract objective clinically interpretable kinematic features from video recordings of individuals performing standard orofacial tasks. This work investigates the analytical and clinical validation of the proposed system to assess the severity of orofacial impairment in clinical groups. Keywords: computer-aided orofacial assessment, neurological disorders, facial kinematics ii

linical validation of the proposed system to assess the severity of orofacial impairment in clinical groups. Keywords: computer-aided orofacial assessment, neurological disorders, facial kinematics ii

Chapter 1 - Introduction Neurological disorders There is a rapid change in the demographic of the world as life expectancy is being prolonged due to several factors such as better healthcare, improved living conditions, and technology advancements [1]. In fact, it is estimated that the older adults aged 60 and over will account for an estimated 22% of global population by year 2050 [2]. Despite the above-mentioned improvements in the life expectancy, neurological conditions have arguably become the most dreaded condition of older adults, with age being the main risk factor for such disorders, including Alzheimer disease (AD), Parkinson disease (PD), motor neuron disease (MND), and stroke [3]. Treating and caring for individuals with such conditions are costly and expected to multiply in the years to come. It is estimated that caring for older adults with neurological conditions already consumed over $600 billion worldwide [4]. As the global population of older adults is on the rise, so is the prevalence of age-related neurological conditions and the need for development of cheap, efficient, scalable tools that are sensitive to the early signs and subtle changes associated with the onset of neurological disorders.

Neurological disorders are brain, cognitive, or behavioural disorders that may affect various human functions such as speaking, limb movements and cognitive/ learning capacity. There are over six hundred known neurological impairments with the global prevalence of these diseases found to be at 10.2% [5]. The most common observed impairments in neurological conditions are impaired motor, sensory, visual, balance, cognition, swallowing function and communication. Motor impairments include weakness or complete paralysis across various muscle groups e.g., in the muscles of upper or lower limbs, trunk muscles as well as muscles of the head and neck, including orofacial muscles. Sensory impairment includes numbness, loss of sensation of touch, pressure, or pain the affected body structures. Cognitive impairment includes, as example, deficits in attention, memory, or visuo-spatial orientation. A common sign of neurological disorders is the loss or altered speech and inability to eat/ swallow normally. Communication impairments may be due to aphasia,

on, memory, or visuo-spatial orientation. A common sign of neurological disorders is the loss or altered speech and inability to eat/ swallow normally. Communication impairments may be due to aphasia,

dysarthria, or apraxia of speech [6]. Dysarthria and apraxia of speech represent the defects to the motor aspects of speech production [6], [7]. Clinical assessment of dysarthria Changes in one's speech might be among one of the early signs of a neurological disorder [8]. Dysarthria - a motor speech disorder - is due to weakness, slowness, or incoordination of the musculature used to produce speech [6], [9]. Dysarthria is the most common occurring speech disorder in neurological conditions, with observed occurrence of 51% in patients with multiple sclerosis, 20-30% in individuals post-stroke, and 50-89% of PD patients [6]. Speech difficulties are observed at disease onset in approximately 30% of patients with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS - aka bulbar onset ALS); while in 80-95% of ALS patients, dysarthria is developed as the disease progresses [10]. Auditory-perceptual assessment methods are considered gold standards for clinical differential diagnosis, assessing severity of symptoms and functional changes as well as making treatment plans and management decisions in dysarthria [11]. For example, a system developed by Darley, Aronson, and Brown established 38 perceptual dimensions defining auditoryperceptual changes in respiration, voice, articulation, prosody, and other aspects of speech to characterize the dysarthria type and localize the lesion site [11].

Auditory-perceptual methods have great advantage such as convenience, economy, and robustness, however, they have been also criticized for being susceptible to various sources of error and biases[12]. This method is heavily subjective and factors such as listener's shifting internal standards, past experiences and area of expertise, and type of rating scale used may become confounding factors [13]. Assessment of orofacial impairments Orofacial muscles are often impaired in neurological diseases. For example, patients with PD can suffer from hypomimia, also known as masked face, in which they have reduced range and speed of facial muscle movements. In order to diagnose this condition, neurologists use the Movement Disorder Society - Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS) to evaluate the presence and the severity of impairments. Section III of the assessment includes items that specifically evaluate the patients' facial expressions [14]. ALS patients have various orofacial

valuate the presence and the severity of impairments. Section III of the assessment includes items that specifically evaluate the patients' facial expressions [14]. ALS patients have various orofacial

and speech difficulties that are observed in 80-95% of patients as the disease progresses with reduced speaking rate and speech intelligibility being identified as the worst aspects of the disease [10]. The ALS Functional Rating Scale - Revised (ALSFRS-R) is a 12-item questionnaire that allows patients to rate their current motor functions including speech, swallowing, and saliva management. It is a highly reliable and easily administrable system that have been used by clinicians to keep track of disease progression [15]. In stroke patients, the orofacial impairments depend on lesion's size and affected cerebral structures. Orofacial impairments may occur due to impairments of the cranial nerves, central nervous system pathways, or motoneuron pool. Stroke patients have demonstrated reduced tongue and lip forces. Facial asymmetry is also common in this clinical group [16]. The cranial nerve examination aims at providing information regarding the focal point of the pathology in the brain or brainstem [17]. It includes examining the functionality of the nerves that innervate muscles of the head and neck. The cranial nerves can be separated into four major groups: the sensory nerves, nerves responsible of eye movements, nerves of oral cavity and superior pharynx, and those responsible of neck movements.

The neurological examination of these nerves includes palpation, check for symmetry and wasting, and testing for weaknesses or paralysis of muscles. The examination includes assessment of structures at rest, during nonspeech activities for example during sustained postures and movements as well as during speech tasks, and in response to reflex testing [18]. However, studies have shown that the assessments performed by the experts are subjective and heavily dependent on the clinician's expertise. Further, there are no agreed upon standards for detection of various disorders and monitoring of disease progression among experts in the field [15], [19], [20]. Using technology to support clinical assessment With the advent of technology and increased availability of sensor-based solution to the consumer marker together with an attempt to overcome the limitations of clinical assessments, instrumentation-based protocols have been explored for the assessment of speech and orofacial impairments [21]-[23]. Some of these instrumented systems include those based on acoustic analyses (e.g., Phonatory aerodynamic system (PAS), nasometer) or kinematic/ movement-based methods (e.g., 3D optical motion capture system, and 3D electromagnetic articulography (EMA)).

d on acoustic analyses (e.g., Phonatory aerodynamic system (PAS), nasometer) or kinematic/ movement-based methods (e.g., 3D optical motion capture system, and 3D electromagnetic articulography (EMA)). Kinematic methods are of particular interest because they allow precise measurements of the movement associated with speech and orofacial functions. The 3D optical motion capture system uses infrared light to track radio reflective markers that are placed on specific facial landmarks to track the facial movement during speech and nonspeech tasks. The facial kinematics data such as range of motion and speed of each articulator provides valuable insight on the performance of the lips, jaw and other facial muscles [22]. This technology however requires a direct line-of-sight between the marker and the camera and cannot be used for the assessment of tongue. As a result, electromagnetic devices, technology based on electromagnetic field that does not require direct line-of-sight, have been used to track and assess the movement of the tongue as well as the lips, jaw and face during speech and orofacial assessments [24]. Studies using EMA have shown promising results and higher sensitivity to the early subtle changes/symptoms in patients relative to healthy adults compared to both clinicians and patients' self-reports [21], [25]-[28].

However, such systems have their own challenges that hinder their adoption into clinical settings including: their complexity and need of further specialized training for clinicians to be able to analyze and understand the data, the high cost associated with the instrumentation, and the long setup time of the sensor-based systems that result in patient fatigue and prolonged assessment time [15]. Solution: 3D cameras The rapid development of 3D sensing technology in the past decade has resulted in many consumer applications. The Microsoft KinectTM was among the first 3D sensing consumer products released that enabled new gaming experience. This product used a depth sensor developed by Primesense that was based on structured light technology. Many other 3D sensing devices shortly followed, some examples were Intel RealSense 3D camera range, Intel® EuclidTM (Intel Corp., Santa Clara, CA, USA), Creative Senz3D (Creative Technology Ltd., Singapore), Asus Xtion Pro Live (AsusTek Computer Inc., Beitou District, Taipei, Taiwan), OptiTrack MoCap (NaturalPoint, Inc., USA), Vicon Infineon 3D Image Sensor (Vicon Motion Systems Ltd., Oxford, UK), and Kinect V2 (Microsoft, Redmond, WA, USA) [29]. As a result, various applications started integrating these technologies, such as gesture and action recognition [30], [31], facial expression analysis [32], and marker-less motion capture [33] just to name a few.

various applications started integrating these technologies, such as gesture and action recognition [30], [31], facial expression analysis [32], and marker-less motion capture [33] just to name a few. Intel RealSense 3D camera was one of the first low-cost, compact with high image quality that was widely adopted in the research community. Intel RealSense 3D camera consists of a depth camera (a projector and a sensor for triangulation) and an RGB camera that is full HD with 1920×1080 pixels resolution. The projector emits 860 nm near-infrared range light, and the IR sensor has a 640×480 pixels resolution with frame rate of 600 fps (1200 fps in half resolution). It is capable of providing a VGA-size depth map at 60 frames per second with 0.125 mm depth resolution, i.e., submillimeter accuracy [34]. There are numerous studies that have explored the use of such 3D cameras for clinical applications. In rehabilitation, such technologies have been considered for enabling patient feedback and correction while performing exercise regimens. For example, Chhor et al. [35] developed a computer game that used Intel RealSense 3D camera to aid upper body rehabilitation by tracking hand gestures. Other examples of applications using Intel RealSense 3D camera by tracking facial expression, movement of joints and skeletal tracking [29], [36], [37]. The depth sensing technology has also been used in facial analysis for tasks such as emotion recognition [38] and providing outcome measures for the assessment of neurological disorders in various clinical populations [39]-[43].

It is the latter application that has a great potential for integration into everyday clinical practice - for in clinic and remote assessments - as well as into clinical trials of pharmaceutical and behavioural interventions. Facial Landmark Detection The development of modern artificial intelligence (AI) and deep learning techniques - specifically, automated video analysis algorithms - afford substantial advances using video cameras for clinical assessment and monitoring. Facial landmark detection and tracking algorithms attempt to automatically detect the location of key facial landmarks on facial images or videos and are the building block of many applications including facial recognition, animation, tracking, expression analysis, and 3D facial modeling. Facial landmark detection is a challenging task for reasons such as diverse facial appearances across subjects, expression and pose changes of a given subject, partial occlusion and environmental factors (e.g., illumination). Therefore, earlier algorithms aimed at detecting landmarks in controlled conditions, for example under a fixed laboratory setting, with fixed head pose and minimal or highly specific facial expressions. In recent

arlier algorithms aimed at detecting landmarks in controlled conditions, for example under a fixed laboratory setting, with fixed head pose and minimal or highly specific facial expressions. In recent

years, more research has been focused on in-the-wild conditions, where algorithms aim at having a robust performance for arbitrary facial expressions, head poses, environmental conditions, etc. [44], [45]. Facial landmark detection algorithms are generally supervised; they use a large set of manually labelled training facial images, from which the AI model learns to locate the same landmarks on future test face images. These models first use a face detector to obtain a rectangular bounding box of the face within a given image. Depending on the model, different number of facial landmarks are outputted (e.g., a 17-point model, 29-point model, or 68-point model) marking the locations of the eyes, nose, mouth etc. on the face. The output is in a form of vector x = (x , . . . , x , y , . . . , y )T where (x , y ), which denotes the pixel values of the ith landmark in the image, N 1 N i i refer to Figure 1-1. Figure 1-1 Output of facial landmark detection algorithm, the pixel values of x and y coordinates are superimposed on the input face image Facial landmark detection models prior to deep learning are categorized into two primary categories, based on either parametric or nonparametric methods. The parametric methods are built based on data belonging to a particular distribution such as a Gaussian mixture model, while

o primary categories, based on either parametric or nonparametric methods. The parametric methods are built based on data belonging to a particular distribution such as a Gaussian mixture model, while

nonparametric models do not assume a distribution. The parametric models have a fixed set of parameters that are tuned based on the input data. Gaussian mixture models and the Active Appearance Models are the most common methods used in this category[46]-[50]. In contrast, the number of parameters in the nonparametric models may vary based on the number and complexity of the training data; these models may still have implicit assumptions or shape constraints built in because of the specifics of the training data. Graphical models such as tree structures and Markov random field methods, in which the location of each facial landmark is optimally solved using dynamic programming, are examples of nonparametric methods [51]. In recent years, deep networks, e.g., convolutional neural network (CNN), have shown superior performance for facial landmark detection. These methods learn the facial shape and appearance variations and the nonlinear mapping from facial images to landmark coordinates. Examples include deep hierarchical face parsing [52] and coarse-to-fine autoencoder networks [53]. In general, deep neural net-based algorithms are divided into two main categories of direct regression methods that directly predict the coordinates of each landmark (e.g. [54]-[58]) and heatmap regression-based methods, where a 2D heatmap is generated for location of each landmark (e.g.

[59], [60]) [61]. Some of the common architectures used as building blocks for either of the above-mentioned approaches are ResNet [58], MobileNet [57], ShuffleNet [56], Hourglass [59], HRNet [62], and CU-Net [60]. The most commonly used architecture is hourglass architecture, initially introduced for pose estimation [63], which is an encoder-decoder network block that can be sequentially stacked [59]. In facial video analysis, recurrent neural networks (RNN) [64] and long short-term memory (LSTM) [65] architectures have been used to minimize frame-to-frame landmark jitter. Based on a review conducted on the state-of-the-art facial landmark detection algorithms developed in recent years [61], some of the best performing models on various datasets include SubpixelHeatmap [66], ADNet [67], and PropagationNet [68]. In SubpixelHeatmap, the authors use local soft-argmax computation to reduce the quantization error of the generated heatmap [66]. The authors for ADNet use the fact that the error in the tangent direction (relative to face boundary) in general has higher value than the error in the normal direction, thus, design the architecture such that it assigns higher weights to normal error [67]. In PropagationNet, the authors note the importance of facial boundary for accurate detection of the facial landmarks [68]. In this approach, convolutional

assigns higher weights to normal error [67]. In PropagationNet, the authors note the importance of facial boundary for accurate detection of the facial landmarks [68]. In this approach, convolutional

blocks transform landmark heatmaps into boundary heatmaps after each Hourglass module and use it as an intermediate attention mask to improve final accuracy predictions [68]. There are several publicly available datasets for training facial landmark detection models [69]-[72]. These datasets can be categorized into those captured in controlled conditions (e.g., in a lab setting with fixed head pose and facial expression) [71], [73], or those captured in uncontrolled environments, i.e., in-the-wild [70]. The latter are often collection of images from websites such as Facebook or Google images. The ground truth facial landmarks are obtained manually by employing workers or crowdsourcing through services such as the Amazon Mechanical Turk. Each image is generally labelled by several workers and the averaged labelled values are taken as final ground truth of landmark positions. The accuracy of the models is measured by computing the distance between the landmark positions predicted by a landmark detection model and the ground truth labels. The computed distance is commonly normalized by the interocular distance and is referred to as the normalized mean error (NME) [61]. Another metric used to report model performance is the failure rate (FR), which denotes the number of images with NME above a given (e.g. 10%) threshold [61].

Facial landmark detection and tracking is an important first step (i.e., a preprocessing step) for many application areas such as face recognition, expression and facial movement analysis, as well as pain detection [74]-[78]. Exploring various algorithms and deep learning architectures and methodologies to improve the tracking accuracy remains an active area of research. Currently, the focus of the field is to balance accuracy and complexity of the models being developed as well as leveraging semi-supervised and unsupervised learning methods in order to take advantage of the abundant unlabeled content online to more effectively train the models [79]. The Face Alignment Network The Face Alignment Network (FAN) [80] is a deep neural network with residual blocks trained on a large pool of real and synthetically expanded images with ground truth facial landmark locations. The FAN architecture is composed of four sequential components, each with an hourglass (HG) architecture [63], in which the bottleneck blocks were replaced with hierarchical, parallel, and multiscale blocks [81]. The outputs are 68 heatmaps of 64×64 dimensions, each of

with an hourglass (HG) architecture [63], in which the bottleneck blocks were replaced with hierarchical, parallel, and multiscale blocks [81]. The outputs are 68 heatmaps of 64×64 dimensions, each of

which estimates the position of a facial landmark. The 2D-FAN was trained using 230,000 images in the training set and was evaluated on 7,200 images in the test set [80]. In previous orofacial analysis studies, FAN was used to extract facial landmarks in clinical populations [82]-[85]. Aligned with findings from Asgarian et al. [86], these studies found the presence of biases resulting in reduced landmark localization accuracy in clinical populations compared to healthy age-matched controls [82]-[85]. As a result, a series of studies addressed the need to reduce such biases via fine-tuning the FAN on data from specific patient groups. Bandini et al. released a publicly available dataset, the Toronto NeuroFace Dataset, from which more than 3,300 manually annotated images of faces from individuals with ALS, post-stroke, and agedmatched controls were used to fine-tune FAN [73]. In follow up studies, Guarin et al. showed that fine-tuning FAN using the clinical data improved the overall model's performance [84] as well as improving the performance of subsequent classifiers using detected landmarks in differentiating clinical groups from healthy controls [87]. Guarin et al. further quantified the amount of clinical data needed to fine-tune and improve the performance of FAN [85].

It was found that fine-tuning the model with as little as 8 annotated images from a single patient resulted in better performance on other individuals from the same clinical group. The best performance was achieved by fine tuning the model with 320 images from 40 patients and using more images from additional patients did not improve the performance further. Overall, the findings showed that fine-tuning FAN using a relatively small sample of clinically represented data improved the performance of the model as well as the subsequent clinical facial analysis task. Validation Framework There have been frameworks developed specifically for the digital health technology development and evaluation [88]. The V3 framework describes three phases for the evaluation of digital biomarkers, namely, verification, analytical validation, and clinical validation [89]. According to this framework, verification refers to validating the hardware and sensors involved in recording the digital measurement. Analytical validation refers to the process of ensuring that the digital biomarker measurements are accurately measuring the intended phenomena. Clinical validation is concerned with evaluating whether the digital biomarker provides meaningful clinical information [89].

al biomarker measurements are accurately measuring the intended phenomena. Clinical validation is concerned with evaluating whether the digital biomarker provides meaningful clinical information [89]. Robin et al. specifically examined this framework and provided further recommendations of how to use this framework for speech related biomarkers and speech-based measures [90]. Using this framework, the verification step involved comparing various technologies and establishing the adequacy of the final technical instrumentation at collecting parameters that can be acceptable digital biomarkers [90]. The verification step of any technology is usually performed by the manufacturer via bench top testing [89]. Analytical step varies depending on the relevant features or methods used to compute the biomarker. This method can entail comparing the new digital biomarker to the existing technical gold standards, obtaining concurrent validation through comparison with other sensors, or through other methods such as cross-validation and independent replication of results. At this phase it is also necessary to analyze and eliminate potential biases in the data as a result of the confounding factors. Clinical validation is the culmination of technology evaluation. At this stage, the technology outputs are compared with clinical gold standards that have been established to detect disease and track its progression. In the assessment of various neurological disorders, some diseases have a clear gold standard while for many others do not.

Therefore, in clinical validation, the choice of reference measure limits the clinical validation as the digital biomarker can only be as good as the measure used to validate it. Clinical validation can be especially challenging, when the used reference measure has limited validity, or lower reliability, resulting in difficulties achieving consistency [90]. Using this framework, engineers, scientists, and clinicians must work together to bring a technology from a design and prototype phase into a clinically utilizable tool [90]. Current Technology: Verification The proposed system used in this thesis consists of Intel RealSense 3D Camera to collect data from patients performing standard orofacial tasks as well as use of the FAN model to automatically extract facial landmarks that are then used to further extract facial kinematics. The performance of both components (the Intel RealSense 3D Camera and FAN model) has been previously studied and verified by their developers as well as the research community. Keselman et al. [91] performed a comprehensive study on the Intel RealSense 3D camera and verified the performance of the system under different test conditions. Performance of FAN was tested on

nity. Keselman et al. [91] performed a comprehensive study on the Intel RealSense 3D camera and verified the performance of the system under different test conditions. Performance of FAN was tested on

some of available open-source datasets and its performance was reported relative to the other facial landmark detection methods, as well as relative to manually annotated facial landmarks [80]. Together the system made from combining these two technologies has the potential to provide clinicians with an objective way of analyzing facial kinematics. However, this system needs to analytically and clinically validated before its adoption as a clinical tool, which are main goals/objectives of this thesis. Current Technology: Analytical Validation The analytical validation process evaluates the generated physiological and/or behavioural metrics in experiments involving human subjects [89]. The Intel RealSense 3D Camera has been previously analytically validated in a study where, Ouni et al. [92] compared Intel RealSense 3D against the sensor-based Electromagnetic Articulography (EMA) and found the tracking error of the lower lip to be low and the system suitable for facial applications that requires facial motion tracking. In another study, Bandini et al. [93] compared the Intel RealSense 3D Camera along with Intraface facial landmark detection and tracking algorithm against the marker-based optical tracking system and found the lips Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) to be between 1-3 mm range. Harkel et al.

[94] have conducted a reliability study comparing Intel RealSense 3D Camera against a highly costly and accurate 3D facial tracking system and found high agreement between the two methods in patients with unilateral facial palsy. The accurate tracking of the facial landmarks is a prerequisite to performing clinically meaningful facial analysis. The development of new and more accurate architectures remains an active area of research and their adoption in developing objective clinical tools is on the rise [95]. However, according to the V3 framework, new analytical validations are needed when there are changes/upgrades to either the software and/or hardware [89]. Thus, as there were no analytical validation studies done previously that uses the Intel RealSense 3D Camera together with the FAN to analyze facial kinematics, the first study of this thesis aims at evaluating this technology. Current Technology: Clinical Validation Clinical validation process evaluates whether the proposed technology provides any valuable clinical insight in a specific context [89]. Clinically, the primary goals of an assessment are to detect clinical features that point to the presence of an abnormality that can lead to disease

luable clinical insight in a specific context [89]. Clinically, the primary goals of an assessment are to detect clinical features that point to the presence of an abnormality that can lead to disease

diagnosis and/ or allows tracking the disease progression over time. Clinical gold standards carry these two important missions - detection/ diagnosis vs. disease tracking [96], [97]. The proposed technology has been previously validated in automatic assessment of facial video recordings during speech and non-speech tasks to distinguishing healthy vs. post-stroke [39], healthy vs. Parkinson's disease [41], and healthy vs ALS individuals [23]. Several facial landmark detection and tracking algorithms have also been clinically validated in other applications such as: automatic pain detection from facial images or videos of participants [98], [99], automatic detection of patients with neurological disorders (e.g. ALS, stroke, PD, and AD) from healthy participants using facial kinematics [39], [42], [100]-[103], and automatic detection of psychiatric conditions (e.g. depression, and schizophrenia) [77], [104], [105]. In all these studies, the main goal was to extract clinically interpretable objective facial features from the landmarks such as facial dynamics and kinematic measures and then to use these measures to detect the presence of disease via classification methods. Another important goal of these measures is to track disease progression over time. For that, correlations with clinical gold standards must be established.

The studies predicting the severity of symptoms have been limited, but important in the effort to clinically validate novel assessment technologies. One study showed that kinematics of facial expressions was able to predict the depression severity estimated via the Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II) [105]. The goal of this thesis is to further clinically validate the use of specific set of deep learning algorithm (FAN and fine-tuned FAN) together with the Intel RealSense 3D camera for use in the clinical setting for detecting severity of orofacial impairments within clinical groups. Summary: Motivation and Gap Detection of most neurodegenerative diseases are highly dependent to the clinicians' experience and are not well standardized [15]. This may lead to delayed diagnosis, and therefore, delayed interventions with reduced possibilities to slow down the rapid reduction of quality of life for the patients. Recent advances in computational power and deep learning algorithms have provided an opportunity to develop potential solutions for automated assessment and early detection of neurological disorders in clinic and remotely, in home settings.

nd deep learning algorithms have provided an opportunity to develop potential solutions for automated assessment and early detection of neurological disorders in clinic and remotely, in home settings. Previous studies have established the Intel RealSense 3D camera's technical verification and shown its high accuracy and performance on bench top experiments [34], [91]. Some studies have also shown analytical and clinical validation of the Intel RealSense 3D camera [29], [43], [73], [94]. Other studies have used facial landmark algorithms to perform clinically relevant facial analysis [41], [42], [73], [106]. However, the combination of the Intel RealSense 3D camera with FAN to perform objective orofacial assessments in clinical groups with facial impairments have not been fully evaluated in a context of V3 framework before. This research thesis focuses on the analytical and clinical validation of the proposed technology (the Intel RealSense 3D camera and FAN) with the aim of advancing its translation into clinical settings. Research Question and Objectives The ultimate objective of this research is to implement an affordable, clinically valid, easyto-use video-based application to support the detection and assessment of orofacial deficits in neurological diseases in order to support clinicians with the disease diagnosis and prognosis.

The objective of this thesis is to analytically and clinically validate the use of an accessible 3D camera and a machine learning-based facial landmark tracking method for extracting kinematic facial features that are representative of the perceptual clinical scores provided by trained clinicians to individuals with orofacial impairments due to neurological disorders (e.g., post-stroke and ALS). In study 1, analytical validation was determined by comparing the kinematic signals extracted from the proposed system (the Intel RealSense 3D camera and FAN) against the gold standard technology - a sensor-based system EMA. In study 2, clinical validity was determined using correlations between the objective kinematic features and perceptual clinical assessments performed by an expert clinician. In the first study (Chapter 2) regarding analytical validation, we hypothesize that the newly proposed system would perform as well as the accepted gold standard method (EMA) with high correlation between the two methods. In the second study (Chapter 3) about clinical validation, we hypothesized that the kinematic features and corresponding perceptual clinical scores - symmetry, range of motion (ROM), and speed - would be significantly correlated with corresponding clinical scores and could be used to predict the severity of oromotor impairment.

Structure of This Document This thesis is structured as follows: Chapter 1 provides a general introduction to the research topic as well as the rationale for this study. It provides an overview of the related literature for the technologies used in this thesis for performing objective orofacial assessments of neurological disorders, including, 3D cameras, AIbased facial detection and tracking algorithms, and application of such algorithms in detection of orofacial impairments. It also lays out the problem, the motivation behind the research, the research framework and objectives, and contributions of this work.

## Chapter 2 - Analytical Validation 2.0 Abstract Facial movements provide valuable information for the assessment of orofacial functions such as speech, swallowing, and facial expressions. Due to the high cost and cumbersome setup procedures, sensor-based technologies have not been adopted in the clinical settings. With the recent advancements in markerless facial tracking algorithms as well as developments of 3D cameras, it has become possible to develop viable clinical applications for the diagnosis and treatment of motor speech disorders. In this study, we assessed the performance of a video-based system composed of a deep neural net facial tracking algorithm coupled with Intel RealSense camera to track the lip movements. The validation was performed on three healthy control participants performing a set of speech and non-speech tasks similar to a routine orofacial assessment conducted in clinical settings. Preliminary results show moderate to strong correlation between this method and the gold standard sensor based electromagnetic articulography. Further algorithm training and tests are needed in order to improve the performance of such system in order to develop an accurate clinical tool for the assessment of orofacial movements. 2.1 Introduction Accurate assessment of orofacial movements is important for the diagnosis and treatment of various neurological disorders.

Changes in the movement of the lips and the jaw have been associated with early signs of oromotor decline, disease progression, and speech intelligibility decline in neurological diseases such as amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), Parkinson's disease (PD), and stroke [107], [108]. Sensor-based technologies such as electromagnetic articulography (EMA) and optical tracking systems are well established methods for the quantitative assessment of orofacial movements. However, such technologies are not clinically feasible due to their high cost, system complexity, and long and tedious setup procedures. As a result, the quantitative analysis of orofacial movement has been limited to research purposes and not integrated into clinical practice in neurology or speech-language pathology [23], [40], [92], [109]. There is a need for an inexpensive, easy-to-use, and clinically feasible orofacial movement tracking system that is able to accurately and quickly quantify patient performance in a clinical setting. Recent advances in computational power and deep learning algorithms coupled with the development of 3D depth cameras have provided an opportunity to develop affordable, markerless, automated, and clinically objective solutions for the assessment of neurological disorders affecting orofacial functions [41], [93], [110], [111].

ded an opportunity to develop affordable, markerless, automated, and clinically objective solutions for the assessment of neurological disorders affecting orofacial functions [41], [93], [110], [111]. The evaluation of 3D cameras as potential tools for facial tracking has begun by the research community. Ouni and Dahmani assessed the performance and precision of two 3D markerless systems (Primesense and Intel RealSense cameras) with respect to an optoelectronic marker-based system in sentence and syllable productions. They used the commercial software Faceshift to track the virtual markers manually placed on the 3D mesh of the facial model in a post-processing step. The outcome of the study proved overall good performance and low RMSE for both markerless systems, with Intel RealSense 3D depth camera being slightly more accurate than the Primesense (RMSE < 2 mm) [92]. Bandini et al. evaluated the accuracy of Primesense depth sensor alongside InteraFace facial landmark tracking software package [112] for 3D tracking of lip movements in various speech tasks with RMSE range of 1-4 mm [93]. In a later study, Bandini et al. were able to discriminate people with PD from healthy control participants using lower lip movement during syllable repetitions under the same video-based settings and confirmed that patients have reduced articulatory kinematics [41]. Bandini et al.

also validated the videobased tracking of jaw movements with Intel RealSense SR300 camera and the RealSense SDK R3 face tracking algorithm against the EMA during speech with the similar accuracy (average RMSE ~ 2 mm) [40]. Other preliminary work has focused on and shown high promise for utilizing opensource video-based facial landmark tracking algorithms in clinical assessments of individuals with orofacial deficits, demonstrating high accuracy (up to 87%) in detecting orofacial impairments [110], [111]. As such, the role of computer vision research to study human face for clinical applications has thrived over the past few years, with many applications in neurological diseases [41], [98], [110], [111]. Despite a significant promise to revolutionize neurological assessment, further research is needed to validate the use of deep learning facial landmark tracking models in assessing orofacial movements. Markerless methods use various computer vision algorithms and machine learning models to retrieve and track facial landmarks in video data. As a result, the quality of the tracking is highly dependent on the tracking model used [92]. The goal of this study was to evaluate the feasibility of state-of-the-art markerless video-based system, as compared to the gold standard EMA for tracking lip movements. The markerless system consisted of a pretrained convolutional neural network (CNN) model for facial landmark tracking as well as a 3D depth camera registered with the video camera in order to translate 2D facial landmarks tracked by the CNN model into 3D locations.

ral network (CNN) model for facial landmark tracking as well as a 3D depth camera registered with the video camera in order to translate 2D facial landmarks tracked by the CNN model into 3D locations.

## 2.2 Method 2.2.1 Experimental Setup and Procedure Three healthy young female volunteers were recruited for the experiment. The demographic details of participants is provided on Table A-1 in Appendix A1. The participants were recorded via 3D (video and depth) camera as well as EMA system while performed a series of speech and non-speech tasks typically used by clinicians in a routine assessment of orofacial and speech function. The tasks were expanded to include conditions (e.g., fast, slow) which are known to enhance performance variability within a speaker. The tasks and conditions were: Sentence repetition: The sentence "Buy Bobby a Puppy" was repeated five times at normal and slow speaking rates, both with normal loudness. Max opening: The mouth was opened to the maximum comfortable range and then closed to the rest position, five times. Four variations of the task were performed, namely, (1) Hold: where participants held the maximum opening for five seconds, (2) Normal: maximum opening followed by closing at a normal speed, (3) Fast: the task was performed as fast as possible, and (4) Asymmetric: opened the mouth asymmetrically to one side (left or right). Max spread: The lips were spread out to the sides without opening, starting from the rest position without a pause (Normal), or with five seconds pause (Hold) at the maximum spread, repeated five times. The recordings were performed in a quiet room, with a single constant and uniform light source placed behind the camera. Participants were seated during the experiment looking straight at the camera. Head movements were monitored and large head movements during experimental recordings were avoided. The EMA sensors were placed using clear surgical tape on the participants' face prior to data acquisition, and the camera was adjusted to be at each participant's eye level. The Wave electromagnetic generator was located on the right side of participants' head, while the 3D camera was placed at a distance of 0.3-0.4 m away from the tip of the nose.

articipant's eye level. The Wave electromagnetic generator was located on the right side of participants' head, while the 3D camera was placed at a distance of 0.3-0.4 m away from the tip of the nose.

2.2.2 Data Acquisition 1) The Wave speech research system (NDI - Waterloo, Ontario, Canada) was used to track lip movements. The Wave system consists of an electromagnetic field generator and sensors that report 5 degrees-of-freedom position information within the set boundaries of the generated EM field with high tracking accuracy (average error = 0.8 mm), thus, making it the "gold standard" for validation of video-based systems [109]. Eight sensors were attached to the participants' faces in the following positions: N1: placed at the intersection of the frontal and nasal bone N2: placed at the tip of the nose. LU (LB): placed at the upper and the vermilion of the upper and lower lips, respectively. LR (LL): placed at the right and left corners of the mouth, respectively. JR (JL): placed on the inferior of the jaw about one quarter of the distance from the gnathion and the right (left) gonion. The 3D position of the sensors over time (trajectories) were collected at the sampling frequency of 100 Hz. Data were recorded and stored using the Wavefront software v.1.1. 2) The video-based markerless system consists of an Intel RealSense SR300 (Intel Corp., Santa Clara, CA, USA) camera that acquires registered color and depth video recordings, as well as the FAN model [80]. FAN is an open-source CNN model used to automatically detect the face, localize 68 facial landmarks, and track them throughout the duration of the videos. The videos were collected at the resolution of 640×480 pixels at 30 frames per second (fps) rate with color (RGB) and depth recording occurring simultaneously. Videos were recorded using custom C++ code.

videos were collected at the resolution of 640×480 pixels at 30 frames per second (fps) rate with color (RGB) and depth recording occurring simultaneously. Videos were recorded using custom C++ code.

2.2.3 Data Analysis 1) Pre-processing: A custom Python script was developed to process and analyze data obtained from each of the two data recording methods used. Because both EMA sensor recordings and the video recordings randomly drop a few data points resulting in inconsistencies within the sampling rate, both data streams were resampled at a uniform rate of 100 Hz using linear interpolation. A low-pass Butterworth filter (8-poles, with cut-off frequency of 15 Hz) was used on the trajectories of the points of interests (landmarks and sensor trajectories) to eliminate jitter. 2) Kinematic measures: As interpretable decisions are crucial in clinical applications, the measures of range of motion and average speed, aligned with typical clinical judgements, were selected for the comparisons. The range of motion (ROM) was estimated vertically and horizontally. The vertical range of motion (VRM) was calculated as the Euclidean distance (in mm) between LU and LB (Wave) and the distance between landmarks 52 and 58 Figure 2-1. The horizontal range of motion (HRM) was calculated as the Euclidean distance between lip corners - RC and LC (Wave) and the distance between landmarks 49 and 55 (refer to Figure 2-1). The velocity signals (in mm/s) were computed as the first derivative of the calculated displacements with respect to time. 3) Validation: The phase correlation in Fourier space [113] was used to align the signals obtained from each method for all the recordings. The zero-lag cross correlation (CC0) was computed to measure the agreement between the two signals obtained from each of the methods for both displacement and velocity features extracted. These measures were calculated for each task and then averaged across the three participants. The tasks were then parsed into individual repetitions and the maximum value of ROM and average speed were calculated for each repetition of the two methods used. The Spearman's correlation (SC) coefficient was then calculated for each task across all the repetitions for each participant and across the three participants.

2.4 Discussion This preliminary study showed high correlations between the EMA and video-based tracking systems with zero-lag cross-correlation values > 0.90 for non-speech tasks and >0.81 for the speech repetition task, when looking at the entire signals. However, the correlation values were smaller when extracting the maximum ROM and average speed values on signals parsed by repetition to obtain discrete, clinically relevant measures. This could potentially be due to the jitter in the facial landmarks obtained using FAN [80], particularly when calculating the correlation between velocity values. In future studies, additional signal processing or improved facial landmarks tracking will need to be considered to reduce frame-to-frame jitter. The correlation values were higher across participants as opposed to within each participant's repetitions of the task. This is due to the fact that the variability of kinematic values were low within each participants' repetitions compared to the trend across all the participant's data. As illustrated in Figure 2-2, there was an expected bias in the signals obtained from the two methods.

This bias was due to the fact that the two methods measured different distances: the ground truth signal (blue) was the distance between two sensor locations, while the FAN tracking signal (red) was the distance between two automatically detected facial landmarks (lip edges). The positions of these reference points were naturally not the same. In addition, some discrepancies between the signals are likely to be due to the sampling rate difference between the two methods, as the video were recorded at a lower sampling rate than the EMA data (30 fps compared to 100 Hz). Future work should aim to record data at the minimum sampling rate of 50 Hz, as recommended by Ouni and Dahmani [92], to enable fine modeling of lip movements. Figure 2-3 shows that the video-based system was able to successfully differentiate between different conditions within individuals (i.e., as expected, the fast condition showed smaller ROM and greater average speed) for the maximum open task. Additionally, it was able to differentiate between speakers, as some participants naturally use larger or smaller movements than other participants. This differentiation is clinically relevant as clinicians are tasked to visually identify the movement ROM and speed. These perceptual judgements have been known to have reduced reliability, and the potential use of video-based methods to distinguish individual differences is highly promising.

movement ROM and speed. These perceptual judgements have been known to have reduced reliability, and the potential use of video-based methods to distinguish individual differences is highly promising.

Clear advantages of the video-based tracking have emerged. First, the acquisition volume for the EMA - determined by the Wave field generator placed near the participant's head - was smaller than the adjustable and larger volume of video-based tracking. In EMA, even small head movements away from the generator's field resulted in the loss of tracking by the sensors. We had to closely monitor the head position of the participants in the EMA field. However, such small movements were accounted for in the video tracking via automated face detection and the detection of facial landmarks so long as the face remained within view of the camera, thus, avoiding the complete loss of data. Furthermore, electromagnetic tracking was limited by the number of sensors placed on the face and highly reduced/limited movement information available for EMA, while the current state-of-the-art facial tracking algorithm tracked large number of facial features (e.g.
68 landmarks). Although we extracted relatively small number of highly straightforward features, the work on the most important feature is still ongoing [42]. In conclusion, this study has demonstrated that the video-based tracking using the FAN [80] has a great potential in tracking facial features relative to the gold standards. Further improvements to its ability to detect facial landmarks needs to establish across a large number of participants with and without neurological disease and across tasks. This ongoing work can help in accurate tracking of facial movements, thus, providing clinicians with guidance in the assessment of neurological disorders.

This ongoing work can help in accurate tracking of facial movements, thus, providing clinicians with guidance in the assessment of neurological disorders. ## Chapter 3 - Clinical Validation Study 3.0 Abstract This study sought to determine whether clinically interpretable kinematic features extracted automatically from 3 dimensional (3D) videos were correlated with corresponding perceptual clinical orofacial ratings in individuals with orofacial impairments due to neurological disorders. 45 participants (19 diagnosed with motor neuron diseases (MND), and 26 post-stroke) performed two non-speech tasks (mouth opening and lip spreading) and one speech task (repetition of a sentence "Buy Bobby a Puppy") while being video recorded in a standardized lab setting. The colour video recordings of participants were assessed by an expert clinician - a speech language pathologist - on the severity of three orofacial measures: symmetry, range of motion (ROM), and speed. Clinically interpretable 3D kinematic features, linked to symmetry, ROM and speed, were automatically extracted from video recordings, using a deep facial landmark detection and tracking algorithm, for each of the three tasks. Spearman correlations were used to identify features that were significantly correlated (p-value < .05) with their corresponding clinical scores. Clinically significant kinematic features were then used in the subsequent multivariate regression models to predict the overall orofacial impairment severity score.

Several kinematic features extracted from 3D video recordings were associated with their corresponding perceptual clinical scores, indicating clinical validity of these automatically derived measures. Different patterns of significant features were observed between MND and post-stroke groups; these differences were aligned with clinical expectations in both cases. The results show that kinematic features extracted automatically from simple clinical tasks can capture characteristics used by clinicians during assessments. These findings support the clinical validity of video-based automatic extraction of kinematic features. 3.1 Introduction Neurological disorders often affect orofacial and speech functions [6]. For example, people post-stroke or living with motor neuron diseases (MND) often experience orofacial motor impairments (i.e., inability to produce facial expressions or perform volitional orofacial movements), as well as dysarthria, a motor speech disorder [114], [115]. Accurate assessment of orofacial speech and non-speech movements becomes important for effective disease detection, tracking progression, and monitoring treatment outcomes [15]. Existing orofacial assessment methods rely on either subjective clinical evaluation performed by experts or on the use of complex and expensive sensor-based techniques, such as electromagnetic articulography (EMA), that are not suitable for integration into current clinical workflows. Emerging video-based methods may offer easy-to-use and cost-effective solutions to

such as electromagnetic articulography (EMA), that are not suitable for integration into current clinical workflows. Emerging video-based methods may offer easy-to-use and cost-effective solutions to

support objective clinical assessment of orofacial motor impairments. The development of 3D video cameras and deep learning algorithms has enabled accurate automatic detection and tracking of pre-defined facial landmarks in facial images [80], which is the first essential step for many clinical facial analysis applications. In order to be adopted into a clinical setting, novel facial tracking technologies must be evaluated within established frameworks for digital biomarker development, such as the FDA BEST (FDA, 2021) criteria and the V3 framework [89]. The V3 framework advocates for a 3-part sequential biomarker evaluation procedure: (1) verification of sensor-level outputs, typically conducted by manufacturers, followed by (2) analytical validation of a novel method with respect to an already-validated technical gold standards, followed by (3) clinical validation to assess utility in a specific clinical population and relative to existing clinical standards/ practices. The technical verification of the facial tracking technologies has been previously well established [29], [34], [91]. The analytical validation of markerless methods has demonstrated promising results as well.

Ouni and Dahmani found that facial tracking methods using two different 3D cameras, namely, Intel RealSense (Intel Corporation, Santa Clara, California, USA) and PrimeSense Carmine (PrimeSense, Tel Aviv, Israel) had good accuracy with respect to optoelectronic tracking, which functioned as a sensor-based ground truth, specifically the Vicon system (Vicon Motion Systems Ltd, Oxford, GB) [92]. Bandini et al. performed an analogous comparison between a 3D camera (Intel RealSense) and an EMA system (NDI Wave Speech Research System - Waterloo, Ontario, Canada), and demonstrated good accuracy of the camera compared to the Wave [40]. Harkel et al. evaluated the intra- and inter-rater reliability and agreement of the Intel RealSense D415 camera against a gold standard stereophotogrammetry imaging system, 3dMD, in patients with facial palsy and found that the 3D camera was a viable option to be implemented in the context of an objective clinical assessment [94]. Taken together, these previous studies have extensively demonstrated the analytical validity of 3D camera-based facial tracking methods. Beyond verification and analytical validation, the technology (both 3D camera and deep learning algorithms) must be evaluated in clinical populations in order to fulfill the third step of biomarker development. Previous work has shown promise for detecting orofacial impairments associated with stroke [116], Parkinson's disease [41], [101], and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) [42] relative to neurologically healthy controls, based on measures of facial kinematics

airments associated with stroke [116], Parkinson's disease [41], [101], and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) [42] relative to neurologically healthy controls, based on measures of facial kinematics

(e.g., velocity, acceleration, range of motion, symmetry, etc.). For example, Bandini et al used a small feature set capturing movement symmetry, speed, and range of motion (ROM) of the lips and jaw to automatically differentiate between post stroke patients and controls with 87% accuracy [116]. In another study, a small number of 3D kinematic features of lip movement were used to discriminate patients with ALS from healthy subjects with nearly 90% accuracy [42]. Neumann et al. used a combination of kinematic features extracted from 2D video recordings together with acoustic features extracted from audio data to distinguish patients with ALS at symptomatic versus pre-symptomatic phases of bulbar disease as well as from the control participants [117]. The focus of previous studies was to investigate video-based methods' use for classifying a group of participants into either the healthy or clinical category. The studies predicting the severity of symptoms have been limited, but important in the effort to clinically validate novel assessment technologies. One study showed that kinematics of facial expressions was able to predict the depression severity estimated via the Beck Depression Inventory-II (BDI-II) [105].

In another study, features were extracted from facial expression images of participants in order to automatically differentiate among five levels of pain using AI-based models evaluated its efficacy on two open-source datasets [118]. Additionally, the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (UPDRS) motor score was predicted using acoustic features extracted from 5875 voice recordings of 42 patients with PD with the final ensemble model predicting the clinical score with 99.6% accuracy [119]. These promising results highlighted the need for more research to clinically validate automated facial analysis technologies for their use as assessment tools, specifically for predicting the clinical scores associated with severity of orofacial impairments. The ability to predict disease severity using kinematic measures extracted from videos would provide evidence of the clinical validity of the technology and support its use to enable tracking disease progression as well as evaluating the effectiveness of intervention programs (e.g., quantifying functional improvement post-stroke). The first objective of this study was to establish clinical validity of a video-based system in assessing the severity of the orofacial impairments by automatically extracting kinematic features from video recordings of individuals with orofacial impairments either due to stroke or MND and comparing them with respect to their corresponding clinical scores (e.g., clinical estimates of symmetry/speed/ROM and kinematic measures of symmetry/speed/ROM). The

ts either due to stroke or MND and comparing them with respect to their corresponding clinical scores (e.g., clinical estimates of symmetry/speed/ROM and kinematic measures of symmetry/speed/ROM). The

second objective was to determine whether a combination of kinematic features was able to predict the overall severity of orofacial deficit in each patient group. We hypothesized that the selected kinematic features and corresponding perceptual clinical scores would be significantly correlated and could be used to predict the overall severity of orofacial impairment.
## 3.2 Methods 3.2.1 Participants Data from 45 patient participants were included in this study: 19 individuals were diagnosed with motor neuron disease (MND, including ALS= 16; primary lateral sclerosis= 1; Kennedy's disease= 2), and 26 individuals who experienced a stroke (PS). We combined all MND patients into the same group as the manifestation of the disease in the orofacial mechanism was expected to be similar (e.g., resulting primarily in kinematic changes due to primarily muscle weakness). Table 3-1 presents the demographic and clinical representation of the samples. The more in-depth clinical details of participants are provided in Table A-2 and Table A-3 in Appendix A2. All participants were cognitively unimpaired (Montreal Cognitive Assessment - MoCA score ≥ 26 [120] and passed a hearing screening at 25 dB for both ears at 500, 1000, 2000, and 4,000 Hz. The MND group was also evaluated via ALS - Functional Rating Scale - Revised (ALSFRSR) [121]. This measure is routinely used by clinicians to evaluate bulbar dysfunction and consists of only 3 questions to address changes in speech, swallowing, and salivation that is rated on a fourpoint ordinal scale with 0 indicating no function and 4 indicating normal function [122]. The study was approved by the Research Ethics boards at the Sunnybrook Research Institute and University Health Network: Toronto Rehabilitation Institute, Toronto, Canada. All participants provided informed consent according to the requirements of the Declaration of Helsinki.
