3.2.2 Clinical Assessment A highly experienced speech-language pathologist specializing in the assessment of adults with neurological diseases (> 10 years of clinical experience) watched the available video recordings and rated them based on clinical measures of ROM, speed, symmetry, variability, and fatigue of facial movements. The rater only knew the diagnosis of the participants prior to performing the assessment and otherwise was blind to all other clinical information (e.g., scores on other clinical assessments, site of onset, disease duration, etc.). Ratings were based on a 5-point Likert scale with 1 indicating normal function and 5 indicating severe dysfunction. ROM, speed, and symmetry scores were used as separate clinical outcomes; they were selected as they constitute salient neuromuscular abnormalities in motor speech disorders [123]. The total score - a sum of all 5 clinical ratings - was calculated and used to represent the overall orofacial deficit severity, with score of 5 indicating normal function and score of 25 indicating the most severe dysfunction (see [73]). To determine the reliability of the clinician scores, the original examinator (intra-rater reliability) and another trained examinator (a certified SLP; interrater reliability) re-scored videos from 15% of the sample (7 participants chosen randomly; 21 recordings).

To assess intra-rater reliability, Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC) estimates and their 95% confidence intervals (CI) were calculated using SPSS statistical package version 23 (SPSS Inc, Chicago, IL) based on a single-rating, absolute-agreement, 2-way mixed-effects model [124]. To assess inter-rater agreement, ICC estimates and their 95% CI were calculated based on a single-rating, consistencyagreement, 2-way random-effects model [124]. A percentage of clinical agreement (percentage of scores within 1-point difference) was also calculated to mitigate the effects of skewed distributions or low variability of the scores in ICC values, if necessary. The intra-rater agreement was strong (ICC = 0.966, CI = [phone-redacted], p < .001; [125]). The inter-rater agreement was also excellent (ICC = 0.996, CI = [phone-redacted], p < .001; [125]). All scores fell within 1-point difference for both intra-rater and inter-rater reliability. 3.2.3 Data collection procedures Video recordings were obtained while participants were seated in front of an Intel RealSense depth camera (SR300 or D400) at approximately 30-60 cm distance away from the face

ta collection procedures Video recordings were obtained while participants were seated in front of an Intel RealSense depth camera (SR300 or D400) at approximately 30-60 cm distance away from the face

at the eye level height. The recordings were performed in a quiet room with a single source uniform light illuminating the participants' face. Each video recording was composed of a pair of color (RGB) and depth videos. Both streams were recorded simultaneously at approximately 50 frames per second at VGA resolution of 640×480 pixels. Participants were asked to perform a set of standard speech and non-speech tasks commonly used by clinicians during orofacial and motor speech examinations to assess the severity of orofacial symptoms and dysarthria. The subjects were asked to avoid head and body movements and look directly into the camera during recordings. The following set of tasks were chosen for analysis as they captured the orofacial movements of mouth and jaw in both horizontal and vertical directions and were affected in both clinical populations in our previous studies [23], [39]: • 10 repetitions of the sentence "Buy Bobby a Puppy" at a comfortable speaking rate and loudness (BBP) • 5 repetitions of maximum jaw opening and closing (OPEN) • 5 repetitions of pretending to smile with tight lips (SPREAD) Furthermore, we recorded 10 seconds of participants resting, while they maintained a neutral facial expression (REST) with their teeth together but not clenched. This was done for distance normalization purposes. A total of 175 video recordings were included in the final analysis: 74 from MND, and 101 from PS. A total of 5 recordings were either missing or excluded. Reasons for exclusion of a video recording for a task were: participant not performing the task (2 cases - in MND group, BBP task), video recording issues, e.g., corruption of either the colour and/or depth stream data of video recordings, or dropping of video frames (2 case - in PS group, BBP task), and failed accurate facial landmark tracking due to poor lighting condition (1 case - in PS group, BBP task). Each of the remaining videos, excluding the data for the REST task, were manually segmented into individual repetitions by a trained research assistant (i.e., 5 segments for the non-speech tasks and 10 segments for the speech task).

cluding the data for the REST task, were manually segmented into individual repetitions by a trained research assistant (i.e., 5 segments for the non-speech tasks and 10 segments for the speech task).

3.2.4 Kinematic Data Analysis Preprocessing A fine-tuned version of the FAN [80] was used to extract 68 facial landmarks from colour videos in 2 dimensions (2D) pixel values and track them throughout the duration of the videos (see Figure 3-1). The 3D positions of landmarks were then calculated by aligning and sampling the depth images at the same pixel coordinates as the landmark points identified on the color images. The 3D real world coordinates of the landmarks (in meters) were calculated using the intrinsic camera parameters together with aligning the depth and colour streams, thus, obtaining 3D coordinates for each landmark, with X representing the lateral axis, Y representing the vertical axis, and Z representing the frontal axis. Figure 3-1 Facial landmarks obtained from the fine-tuned FAN. The landmarks used for extracting kinematic features are named and highlighted in light blue dots. The red dashed lines are the kinematic displacement measures extracted from specific facial landmark pairs. It has been previously shown that the state-of-the-art deep neural facial landmark detection models do not perform well when used on faces of older adults or patients with orofacial deficits [73], [83]. To address this issue, the model was previously fine-tuned using 4340 images extracted from video recordings of individuals from clinical populations as well as healthy older adults, some of which were of a subset of the individuals in the present study, performing orofacial tasks [126]. The normalized Root Mean Squared Error (NRMSE) of the fine-tuned model was reported

er adults, some of which were of a subset of the individuals in the present study, performing orofacial tasks [126]. The normalized Root Mean Squared Error (NRMSE) of the fine-tuned model was reported

to be 4.7 ± 0.7 %, 6.2 ± 1.7 %, and 5.7± 1.1 % for healthy control, post-stroke group, and ALS group, respectively [126]. The fine-tuned FAN model has been previously used to successfully detect oromotor impairments associated with various neurological disorders [73], [83]. To check the model's accuracy, a video file was generated of participants performing each task with the location of 2D landmarks superimposed on their faces. Each video was viewed to ensure adequate detection and tracking of facial landmarks of interest. Out of 68 facial landmarks, 10 landmarks from lips and jaw were considered in this study. They included: Forehead (FH), left medial canthus of eye (LE), right medial canthus of eyes (RE), upper vermillion lip border at mouth centre (UL), lower vermillion lip border (LL) at mouth centre, left oral commissure (LC), right oral commissure (RC), centre jawline (CJ), left jawline (LJ), and right jawline (RJ). To remove tracking noise and jitter, the trajectories of the landmarks of the interests were lowpass filtered with an 8-pole Butterworth filter with cut-off frequency of 12 Hz [73], [127]. Kinematic Features Similar to previous studies, facial landmarks were used to derive clinically interpretable and relevant kinematic features [42], [84]. These features are summarized in Table 3-2.

Features were selected to correspond to clinical judgements of ROM, speed and symmetry by the speech language pathologist. A total of 20 kinematic features were linked to ROM, 24 to speed, and 9 to symmetry. All the features have been used in previous studies [23], [101]. ROM Features The 3D Euclidean distances between a set of facial landmarks were calculated to establish lip opening, UL - LL; lip spread, RC - LC; movement of LL with respect to the FH reference point; movement of RC with respect to RE; and movement of LC with respect to LE. In addition, the total movement of the mouth was modelled by a sum of the areas made by two triangles defined by lip landmarks {RC, UL, LL} and {LC, UL, LL}. Lip spread as well as other RC/LC measures were established only for SPREAD, where horizontal movements were predominant. Since the jaw rarely moves during SPREAD, none of the jaw-based features were considered for this task. All the calculated distance features were normalized with respect to the corresponding values

t. Since the jaw rarely moves during SPREAD, none of the jaw-based features were considered for this task. All the calculated distance features were normalized with respect to the corresponding values

calculated in the REST condition. After this normalization, ROM was determined as the maximum value of each of these distances/areas, as well as the cumulative sum path length. This was done for each repetition. Speed Features The first derivative of the above path distances with respect to time were calculated to represent the velocity of the lips and jaw movements. The maximum, minimum, and average values were considered. Symmetry Features The symmetry of movement was characterized by (1) comparing the motion of the left and right corners of the mouth with respect to the reference points LE and RE and (2) comparing the areas on the right and left triangles defined by {RC, UL, LL} and {LC, UL, LL}, respectively. The absolute differences, ratios, and concordance correlation coefficient (CCC [128]; as a measure of agreement of two signals) between the computed values of the distances and mouth areas on the right and left sides were calculated. Smaller CCC values corresponded to asymmetry during facial movements. Similarly, the larger deviation of right/left ratios away from 1 indicated more asymmetrical facial movements. 3.2.5 Statistical Analyses The features described above were calculated for individual repetitions of all tasks. Prior to further analyses, outliers were removed from the data using the inter-quartile range (IQR) method [129]. In this method, the IQR of the repetitions were calculated, then multiplied by 2.5, and added/subtracted from the third/first quartiles. The values per repetition of each task that fell outside this range were considered outliers and were eliminated from the set. In total, 4.7% of the repetitions were discarded as outliers (n=51). The arithmetic mean of kinematic feature values were calculated cross the remaining repetitions per subject, per task. All subsequent analyses were conducted separately for each task (OPEN, SPREAD, BBP) and group (MND vs. post-stroke). The Spearman correlation coefficients were calculated between each kinematic feature and their corresponding perceptual clinical scores. The Spearman rho (r) is used with ordinal data and measures the degree of monotonic relationship between two variables; here, in accordance with accepted guidelines, values between 0 to ±0.20 were considered as negligible, ±0.21 to ±0.40 as weak, ±0.41 to ±0.60 as moderate, ±0.61 to ±0.80 as strong, and ±0.81 to ±1.00 as very strong [130]. The significant level for each correlation was set to 0.05 to reflect that these correlations were primarily used as a screening method for subsequent regression analyses.

±1.00 as very strong [130]. The significant level for each correlation was set to 0.05 to reflect that these correlations were primarily used as a screening method for subsequent regression analyses.

[TABLE omitted: 5 rows x 5 cols]

Kinematic features with statistically significant correlations were included in the subsequent multivariable linear regression models with the total orofacial clinical score as the outcome. The Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) with threshold of greater than 10 was used to assess the collinearity between covariates [131]. If the VIF for the variables were higher than the set threshold, the features with highest VIF were eliminated iteratively until the remaining variables all had VIF values less than 10. The normality of residuals was tested by plotting the Q-Q norm of the residuals. The linearity of fitted regression models was also evaluated by plotting the residual values against the kinematic features data. The adjusted R2, adjusted for the number of predictors, was reported for the final fitted regression models.

3.4 Discussion In this exploratory study, we investigated clinical validity of a 3D camera and vision-based facial tracking algorithm as an objective assessment tool for patients with neurological disorders - MND and PS. A relatively small, clinically interpretable lip and jaw kinematic feature set was delineated from the outset; the features were chosen to conceptually represent the measures used by clinicians to assess orofacial deficits during the oromotor examinations of non-speech and speech tasks. The study revealed that subsets of kinematic features were significantly correlated with their corresponding clinical scores for each clinical group. The sets of statistically significant features that were able to explain a significant portion of data variability in the overall severity scores in most models were unique for each of the clinical subgroups. Overall, the findings demonstrated the potential of automated video-based systems for extraction of kinematic features in the context of orofacial neurological assessment and established their initial validity as clinical tools. 3.4.1 Facial kinematics in MND and PS In total, there were 16 statistically significant correlations (out of 50, 32%) across all three tasks for the MND group. In the PS group, 23 individual correlations (8 for SPREAD and 15 for BBP) of kinematic features with clinical scores were significant (46%).

Although less than 50% of correlations were significant, and those that were significant ranged between 0.40 and 0.74 (moderate to good), the results were overall encouraging. Smaller correlations and model fits have been expected based on previous literature exploring relationships between kinematics and other measurement domains, including perceptual and acoustic (see Lee et al., 2018; Rong et al., 2012; Teplansky et al., 2022). However, the findings of this study aligned strongly with those of previous work with regards to facial kinematics and oromotor phenomena in both MND and PS groups. In the MND group, our findings were consistent with previous literature describing abnormal jaw and lip movements [42], [127], [135], [136]. MND has been characterized by weakness in the orofacial musculature [137] and, expectedly, ROM and speed measures were noted to be substantially more affected by MND than symmetry (see Shellikeri et al., 2016). As a result, we observed significant

rofacial musculature [137] and, expectedly, ROM and speed measures were noted to be substantially more affected by MND than symmetry (see Shellikeri et al., 2016). As a result, we observed significant

correlations between the clinical scores indicating the impairment severity and kinematic features primarily related to ROM and speed. In the PS group, facial paresis, resulting in facial asymmetry with a unilateral muscle impairment of lower half of the face, causing asymmetric smile and other facial gestures, is one of the most common impairments following a stroke [116], [139]. As anticipated, the symmetry features played a prominent role in the PS group, and our finding showed significant correlations between kinematically assessed symmetry and the corresponding symmetry clinical scores. In both clinical groups, the agreement between clinical characteristics sought by experts to describe the unique clinical representation of orofacial deficits and corresponding kinematic features supported the overall impression of clinical validity of the video-based movement assessment. 3.4.2 Task differences Differences also emerged in this study between tasks. In the MND group, kinematic measures of ROM and speed were linked to the corresponding clinical scores in non-speech OPEN and SPREAD tasks, but not in the speech task-BBP. The findings for non-speech tasks were anticipated based on past research [42]. Both tasks are classified as maximum performance tasks, yet clinically they serve different purposes.

While OPEN primarily is used to judge the integrity of jaw depressor/elevator muscles, SPREAD is used to judge the integrity of intrinsic facial muscles. Previously, kinematic features extracted from lower lip and jaw in SPREAD and OPEN were used to differentiate patients with ALS from healthy control participants with 83% and 72% accuracy, respectively [42]. The findings regarding the speech task were somewhat unexpected, as previous studies had shown BBP to be highly sensitive to the presence of the motor neuron disease [23], [127]. Current results for BBP may be due to the challenges associated with clinical (visuo-perceptual) assessment of speech versus non-speech movements by SLPs. BBP is a relatively fast task and as such it might be more challenging for clinicians to rank its clinical characteristics (ROM, speed, and symmetry) on a 5-point scale. Further, only 2D colour video recordings (no audio) were viewed by the expert for clinical judgement of BBP, which might have resulted in a loss of some information (e.g., rate/slowness) essential for forming the overall clinical impression. Limited sample size and high variability (due to factors such as heterogeneity of disease presentation) may have also contributed to these results.

sential for forming the overall clinical impression. Limited sample size and high variability (due to factors such as heterogeneity of disease presentation) may have also contributed to these results. In the PS group, results for the vertical opening (OPEN) contrasted with the horizontal opening (SPREAD) and speech (BBP) task. None of the kinematic features were correlated with clinical scores in OPEN, while sets of features were correlated with the clinical scores in SPREAD and BBP. The lack of correlation among features and the clinical scores in OPEN may be due to the task being mainly used to assess the integrity of the jaw (i.e., cranial nerve V) and not facial muscles, which might be differentially affected post-stroke [116]. The SPREAD task showed more notable effect as it intended to reveal the integrity of facial muscles (i.e., cranial nerve VII). Although relatively mild, this type of impairment predominated in our PS sample. Facial features during BBP also revealed a link with the clinical symmetry score in these patients. Similar results for SPREAD and BBP with respect to their sensitivity to orofacial impairment post-stroke were reported in previous work (Bandini, Green, Richburg, et al., 2018). Overall, with the exception of BBP task in the MND group, task effects observed clinically and kinematically in this study demonstrated results consistent with previous research and, by extension, supported the potential for clinical validity of the tested assessment method.

3.4.3 Modeling overall orofacial impairment severity A combination of kinematic features was able to predict the overall orofacial impairment severity score in each clinical group and some tasks. This was important to examine as the overall severity score combined clinical impressions across different specific observations that might be difficult to rank separately and individually. In the MND group, significant models were noted for OPEN and SPREAD. All but one individual variable (LL_Max) contributed to the overall model in OPEN explaining a substantial degree of variability of the total clinical score. The value of the coefficients generally suggested that as the severity increased, the values of the ROM and speed of the lips and jaw were reduced. The SPREAD model was significant, but none of the kinematic variables significantly contributed to explaining the model's overall variance. This may suggest that the SPREAD was not an ideal task for feature extraction in this group, as previous research suggested that the greatest impairment in MND affects the vertical movement plane of the jaw and lips [136]. In the PS group, in contrast with MND, most of the features were significant in the regression model for SPREAD, with kinematic features explaining over half of the variance in the

jaw and lips [136]. In the PS group, in contrast with MND, most of the features were significant in the regression model for SPREAD, with kinematic features explaining over half of the variance in the

data. The results of this study are promising as the variability of the kinematic measures within and in between subjects in the study were considerable. The value of the coefficients revealed that as the severity increased, the lip movement became more reduced and asymmetrical. In BBP task, total mouth area and the maximum mouth opening velocity were the two statistically significant features with the model being able to explain substantial amount of the variance in the data. These features align with clinicians' judgement as they assess the overall mouth movement and speed of movement during a fast task such as BBP. Overall, the features related to symmetrical movement of the mouth demonstrated to be of the most importance aligned with the clinical perceptual assessment of this clinical group, further strengthening the validity of this method for future automatic objective assessment. 3.4.4 Limitations and future directions This work had several limitations. The primary limitation was a relatively small sample size and differences in sample sizes of the two clinical groups, limiting our ability to fully compare group results. Another limiting factor was a relatively small severity range in both clinical samples. We capitalized on the greater variability available in our samples based on specific tasks instead.

In addition, the kinematic features extracted were averaged across the repetitions for each subject per task and thus variability of the facial movements during each repetition of the task were not considered. Future work will extend on the results obtained in the study, focus on gathering data from a larger/ balanced groups of participants with more severe orofacial impairments as well as developing predictive models using timeseries of facial landmarks instead of averaging kinematic feature values across repetitions. This work will be essential to develop and integrate modern digital technologies into clinical care as well as outcome measures for clinical trials [88]. The present work had limitations to acknowledge and to address in future studies. The primary limitation was a relatively small sample size and differences in sample sizes of the two clinical groups, limiting our ability to fully compare group results. 3.5 Conclusion The result of this study showed that the automatic extraction of facial kinematic features from facial video recordings of patients performing standard orofacial assessments is a viable solution for an objective and continuous assessment of facial function. Here we demonstrated encouraging support for the validity of our automated assessment method for detecting the severity of kinematic facial impairments. Development of such systems and their validation with larger datasets holds great promise for the development of clinical tools that can help clinicians objectively assess oromotor and speech disorder and track progression of neurological diseases.

Chapter 4 - General Discussion Need for Automatic Objective Orofacial Assessments The goal of this thesis was to validate the use of 3D camera together with deep facial landmark tracking algorithms (the FAN and the fine-tuned FAN) to extract orofacial features aimed at providing objective clinically interpretable measures/ biomarkers to guide clinical decision making in diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment management of neurological disorders. Neurological disorders are heterogenous in their clinical presentation, making them challenging to diagnose [140]. Furthermore, the lack of standards to follow in the clinical setting, and subjectivity of the existing methods used by experts can result in delays in obtaining diagnosis and delayed interventions and treatments. As a result, the clinical management and patient experience is negatively impacted [141]. Studies have shown that subtle changes in speech and orofacial movements may be early signs of disease [8]. The use of markerless video-based technologies can provide clinicians with objective automatic analysis of facial movements and speech patterns that are non-invasive, cost effective, and possibly sensitive to subtle changes that are not noticed by the clinicians [127].

In fact, video-based technologies have been used in orofacial assessments to differentiate between groups of individuals with various neurological diseases and healthy control participants [39], [40], [43], [106], [127], as well as differentiating among various stages of neurological diseases [117], [127]. Therefore, development of such technologies can help support clinicians in the assessment, diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment planning processes. AI-Vision based Automatic Assessment Machine learning and deep learning techniques are the dominant methods used in automatically analyzing videos in the field of computer vision. New architectures with higher accuracy are being actively developed in this fast-paced research community. While the current top performing algorithms may report high accuracy relative to manual human annotators on the dataset that they were trained on, they perform worse on datasets that they were not trained on [142]. The other limitation of such algorithms is their less-than-optimal performance in cases of occlusions such as glasses, facial hair, extreme poses, or diverse environmental conditions such as

on [142]. The other limitation of such algorithms is their less-than-optimal performance in cases of occlusions such as glasses, facial hair, extreme poses, or diverse environmental conditions such as

lighting [45]. These events are extremely difficult to control, and they can compromise the data quality. In our work, we opt to perform data collection in a semi-structured environment, when a research assistant or clinician instructs a patient/ participant to avoid occlusion and maintain optimized parameters for the duration of the recording session. In addition, most of these models are supervised methods that are trained on large amount of labelled data that may be inherently biased. In particular, landmark detection and tracking algorithms are mainly trained on the online available pictures of faces, which mostly consist of young subjects with light skin tone [143]. A thorough analysis of available datasets have shown that these corpuses are indeed sex/ gender, race, and age imbalanced [142]. Therefore, as expected, off-the-shelf models are often biased towards older participants, darker skin tone, and patient groups [86], [102], [144]. Training such algorithms on clinical data alone is not practical and unrealistic as the new state-of-the-art deep learning algorithms require large corpus of training data (in the order of thousands to millions of samples).

Since most clinical studies have relatively small sample size (~10-150 participants per class) and strict restrictions on data sharing broadly among the research community, fine-tuning based on these samples remains challenging. Nevertheless, technical solutions can be found to address some of the limitations and the inherent biases in these algorithms. Guarin et al. have shown that using examples from only 40 subjects in a controlled environment may be sufficient to improve the performance of the FAN model that was previously shown to be biased towards the older and/or clinical participants [145]. Fine-tuning is one of the effective methods of de-biasing existing state-of-the-art AI models [145]. Taati et al have also successfully applied this method to algorithms for facial tracking landmarks and have shown improved performance on tracking of facial features of older adults with dementia [102]. AI-based applications should be evaluated for their validity prior to their adoption. This work used the well-established V3 framework [89] for the validation of digital biomarkers to evaluate the validity of the proposed video-based technology. As the technical verification of the used technology was previously established [34], [91], this work aimed to further validate this technology both analytically, relative to a technical gold standard, and clinically, relative to an assessment by an expert clinician.

Ultimately, robust clinically verified AI-based models need to be externally validated on a clinical cohort that adequately represents the target patient population from various collection sites in order to reduce the risks of overfitting and biases [146].

eed to be externally validated on a clinical cohort that adequately represents the target patient population from various collection sites in order to reduce the risks of overfitting and biases [146]. Analytical Validation In the first study of this thesis, the proposed technology was analytically validated relative to the gold standard highly precise and accurate technology - EMA. It was a pilot study that involved data collection from three healthy adults performing three standard assessment tasks (OPEN, SPREAD, BBP) at varying conditions (e.g. fast, slow, normal, and asymmetrical). The original intent was to expand the data set to more participants; however, the data collection was impacted by the COVID pandemic, and the study had to be stopped due to the inability to move to a remote data collection mode. This study is currently in progress - a data collection for 20 participants has been completed and will be analysed elsewhere. The kinematic features extracted in the pilot study, namely ranges of motion/ speeds etc. associated with mouth opening and lips spreading during speech and non-speech tasks were compared between the two technologies using the zero-lag cross-correlations and Spearman correlations. The zero-lag cross correlation measure showed strong agreement between the two methods when comparing the entire kinematic signal per repetition. However, Spearman correlations of the calculated averages and maximum values of kinematic features showed mostly moderate correlations. Two conclusions were drawn from the results of this preliminary study.

First, the 30 frames rate per second of the video camera can be a limiting factor since fast facial movements may not be captured. Ouni et al. recommended a minimum of 50 frames per second to ensure capturing all the critical information for fast movements such as speech [92]. Second, the jitteriness of the landmarks in the video resulted in "noisy" distance signals and high variability of kinematic values extracted. In addition, this study demonstrated that there was high variability in the range of orofacial kinematics of orofacial movements performed by participants both within and in-between subjects. The high variance needs to be considered before making conclusions regarding the results of studies using such technologies. Finally, the strengths of the correlations were also dependant on the participants. The highest correlations were obtained on the participant with the lightest skin tone, which is aligned with the findings of previous studies on the inherent biases of deep facial landmark detection algorithms [86], [102], [106], [144], [145]. This study also revealed some of the challenges and limitations of the proposed system. The RealSense video camera sometimes dropped video frames, which resulted in overall lower

[106], [144], [145]. This study also revealed some of the challenges and limitations of the proposed system. The RealSense video camera sometimes dropped video frames, which resulted in overall lower

video quality. A new recording software was developed in the lab to address this problem and inform the researchers if a specific recording needed to be repeated, based on the number of dropped samples and overall low recording quality. The off-the-shelf FAN model used in this study showed high sensitivity to environmental factors such as variation in lighting as well as skin tone of participants. As such, fine-tuning procedures were established to improve FAN's performance and were used in the clinical validation study and elsewhere in our research. The main limitation of this study was the small sample size (n=3). Ongoing work focuses on full sample (n=17) evaluation of landmark detection and tracking algorithm on the data collected from EMA, 3D RealSense camera, and a laptop webcam [147]. The result of this study will be reported elsewhere and will include measures of inter-method agreement, test-retest consistency, and reliability of such technologies for performing automatic objective orofacial assessments. Clinical Validation In the second study, the proposed technology was clinically validated in the MND and Post-stroke groups. Participants performed the same tasks as first study (OPEN, SPREAD, BBP) while being recorded in a controlled lab environment with a 3D camera.

In this study, a fine-tuned model of the landmark detection and tracking algorithm on clinical data [85], [145] was used to automatically extract clinically relevant kinematic features from lips and jaw movements. A set of features in each clinical group showed moderate to good correlation with the perceptual clinical scores given by the expert clinician. The statistically significant features within each task and clinical subgroup were representative of clinical characteristics anticipated by the group. The regression models built by the significant features were able to explain a good amount of variability within the data. High within and between subject variability was also observed in the extracted kinematic features in each of the categories of the severity scale, which highlights the challenges associated with heterogeneity of clinical characteristics and presentations across clinical populations. A recent longitudinal study of post-stroke participants established a high internal consistency and reliability of such technologies with the ability to identify individual and taskdependent differences over time [148].

al study of post-stroke participants established a high internal consistency and reliability of such technologies with the ability to identify individual and taskdependent differences over time [148]. In this study, we only focused on three out of the five available clinical scores; namely, ROM, speed, and symmetry. There were no objective kinematic measures defined/extracted for the two remaining scores of variability and fatigue. However, these measures are also important in characterizing neurological diseases. For example, individuals with ALS have reported fatigue as the most prevalent symptom occurring in 83% - 90% of patients compared with 20% in healthy control group [149]. Future studies should define objective and clinically interpretable measures for fatigue and variability sub-scores and include them for the full analysis. An example of a clinically interpretable objective measure to consider for fatigue is the rate of change in a given kinematic feature over consecutive repetitions of a task. It is expected that larger clinical scores of fatigue would translate to larger differences between kinematic features from early repetitions to final repetitions. Similarly, one can examine the standard deviation of a given kinematic feature over multiple repetitions as a potential marker of the variability sub-score. Finally, this study provided some insights on the usefulness of different assessment tasks for quantifying different aspects of orofacial impairments.

For example, SPREAD is mainly used to assess symmetry of the horizontal lip motion, while OPEN assesses the vertical symmetry as well as the range of motion of the jaw and lips. Understanding the clinical importance and interpretation from each assessment task can help understand and validate such clinical tools as long as the automatic kinematic features extracted remain clinically interpretable and relevant. This study showed that predicting the severity of clinical rating scales is challenging and may be limited by 1) the small sample size, and 2) the skewed distribution of the data across severity scale. Future studies should focus on gathering data from participants with more severe orofacial impairment in order to balance the dataset and have representative data in all the categories. Overall, this study provided evidence for validity of such technologies as objective assessment tools for orofacial movements. Conclusion The overall goal of this project was part of an effort to help the translation of novel videobased technologies capable of providing objective kinematic measures into a useful clinical tool allowing for more accurate assessment of orofacial deficits within neurological disorder populations. The combined results indicated that such technology can be used to automatically extract clinically meaningful and objective features that clinicians can incorporate into their workflow to make more informed and data-driven decisions. Limitations and Future Directions Upgrading the facial detection and tracking algorithm In present work, FAN [80] and fine-tuned FAN [85] models were used for facial landmark detection and tracking purposes. FAN is a relatively old model (proposed in 2017) in the domain of deep facial tracking architectures, and lacks testing on widespread face landmark datasets [61]. Current state-of-the-art models have reported higher performance and have been trained on larger and more diverse datasets than FAN. For example, PropagationNet, ADNet, and SubpixelHeatmap are more recent architectures that have achieved top performance on different datasets [61].

They are all based on the similar heatmap architecture as FAN and use Hourglass for their backbone with their own unique improvement; PropagationNet introduced a new face boundary attention module, ADNet separates normal and tangent errors, while SubpixelHeatmap focuses on reducing heatmap quantization error [61]. The higher performance of these algorithms on datasets used for training and testing FAN as well as more challenging datasets [61] suggest their superior performance and higher facial landmark detection and tracking on the clinical data collected for these studies. Using the methods in [85] and clinical orofacial datasets such as [73], these models can be further fine-tuned to more accurately track the facial features in clinical populations. Increasing the performance of landmark detection and tracking algorithms will directly translate to extracting more accurate kinematic features and thus development of more reliable and accurate tool for assessing orofacial movements. Using all the spatial and temporal data from facial landmarks The current studies used the values of kinematic features averaged across multiple repetitions which provided a single discrete value per kinematic feature, task, and participant. This results in loss of potentially useful information within each repetition as well as more broadly looking at the values of features across multiple repetitions. For example, useful information

nt. This results in loss of potentially useful information within each repetition as well as more broadly looking at the values of features across multiple repetitions. For example, useful information

regarding fatigue and variability clinical scores can be obtained by looking at the changes of the kinematic measures across multiple repetitions in tasks such as sentence repetition. Future work may explore using time series data of kinematic features rather than single values to potentially capture more clinically relevant features. For example, in a study using an LSTM architecture on the time series of facial landmarks data obtained from participants expressing specific emotions in front of camera was used to distinguish between individuals with Parkinson disease and healthy controls [100]. In this study LSTM showed promising results in analyzing sequential video data to guide clinicians with diagnosis of neurological diseases. The main limitation of this study was small sample size [100]. In the future, as more clinical data becomes available, such architectures can be used to derive clinically meaningful features that are sensitive to temporal changes within multiple repetitions. Multimodal data This work only considered 3D video data (colour and depth). Recent studies have shown that multimodal data (both video and audio) can further improve the predictive power of such algorithms [117], [150]-[153]. For example, Neumann et al. demonstrated that combining acoustic signals obtained from the audio signal (i.e.

features obtained from acoustic analysis) together with facial kinematic features results in increase predictive power of these such algorithms [117]. Using a set of features extracted from video as well as audio signals, the trained algorithms showed promising results in differentiating between healthy controls, pre-symptomatic ALS, and bulbar ALS, thus, demonstrating the utility of incorporating various speech and facial metrics to assist early diagnosis and monitoring of ALS [117]. Similarly, the future work of this research should consider extracting clinically valid speech features from audio recordings of the speech tasks to potentially increase the predictive power of the regression models. Collecting Longitudinal Data These technologies have a great potential for diagnosis and prognosis of neurological disorders as larger volumes of longitudinal data, accounting for objective inter and intra subject variability, is being gathered [154]. Current clinical practice suffers from one-shot assessments, where patients see clinicians once every few months or even years [149]. The performance of the patient may not provide a true picture of their performance due to the day-to-day variability of the

ts, where patients see clinicians once every few months or even years [149]. The performance of the patient may not provide a true picture of their performance due to the day-to-day variability of the

disease characteristics [154]. This is of particular importance in neurological disorders in which the disease representation itself is heterogenous and patient's clinical characteristics may vary from day-to-day [139], [154]. Therefore, collecting repeated assessments reduce intra-participant's variability while increasing the power of the study [154]. In addition, longitudinal study design can be more efficient, cost effective, and result in more robust model selection process [155]. Previously longitudinal orofacial and speech home-based assessments in neurological disorders resulted in characteristics of disease progression and/or response to treatment interventions [96]. The future studies will focus on collecting data longitudinally and modelling progression in addition to symptoms' severity.

Deniz Jafari PhD Candidate at University of Toronto Cedar Valley, Ontario, Canada Summary I am a PhD candidate in Biomedical Engineering with emphasis in Robotics at the University of Toronto (UofT) under the supervision of Dr. Alex Mihailidis. My current research focuses on developing a modular, gamified robotic platform to enhance motivation and engagement in stroke rehabilitation. I am passionate about developing affordable, practical healthcare solutions that significantly improve quality of life. Experience 6 years Doctoral Student September 2022 - Present (3 years) Toronto, Ontario, Canada My current work involves developing a modular, gamified robotic rehabilitation system to improve engagement and motivation in stroke rehabilitation for upper limb impairments. MSc Student September 2019 - August 2022 (3 years) My research as a MSc student in the Rehabilitation Sciences Institute at UofT concentrated on validating the use of deep learning algorithms in order to develop potential solutions for automated assessment and early detection of neurodegenerative disorders. I used state-of-the-art deep facial tracking algorithms to automatically extract clinically interpretable objective features from video recordings of facial movements, and validated the method against the perceptual clinical scores provided by expert clinicians. Sunnybrook Research Institute My graduate MHSc thesis focused on developing and validating a navigation workflow for the vertebral radio-frequency ablation (RFA) of spinal metastases. The implemented software combines pre- operative and intra-operative medical imaging data with RFA treatment planning to facilitate the optimal delivery of heat to ablate tumours within the vertebral bone structure, while minimizing the potential for damage to adjacent critical structures. This project required an integration of wide range of technologies such as cone beam CT scanner, optical tracking camera and markers, RFA system, and software platforms to track and visualize the RFA procedure. The final developed navigation workflow was validated in animal studies. Lower Mainland Biomedical Engineering Graduate Engineering Student Intern May 2018 - September 2018 (5 months) Vancouver, Canada Area Developed a process to standardize the capital replacement and procurement process across the province by developing and utilizing a systematic data driven asset prioritization tool allowing for easier, faster, and more confident decision making process Undergraduate Trainee September 2015 - April 2016 (8 months) Sunnybrook Research Institute My undergraduate thesis work involved developing a methodology to build a micro- catheter optimally designed for chronic total occlusion percutaneous coronary interventions. The aim of this project was to design a catheter that would allow for the insertion of imaging assembly (such as IVUS or OCT) as well as the guidewire in order to provide image guidance during revascularization procedures, and hence improve procedural outcomes. In order to achieve the project goal, I compiled a list of design requirements, developed various designs, evaluated them against the requirements, prototyped several catheters, and performed bench-top experiments to test for their feasibility. My research introduced me to the challenges faced while developing biomedical technologies intended to be biocompatible and safe for human use, which I believe is paramount to producing clinical focused and ground breaking medical devices. Medical Device Design Assistant February 2015 - September 2015 (8 months) During my co-op term working as a medical device design assistant, I learnt about the development and validation process of miniature medical devices for the minimally-invasive treatment of cardiovascular diseases. This industrycollaborative research work included creating prototypes of catheters, developing and conducting experiments to optimize design parameters, and coordinating the needed material purchases. Furthermore, I was responsible for designing specific features of novel intracardiac imaging devices. My most notable achievement during this term was developing a new, patent pending, method for assembling intracardiac catheters. My other significant contributions included conducting a literature review of catheter tracking technologies, prototyping a cardiac pacing catheter (tested in vivo in pig), and developing a methodology to construct catheter sheaths in house. Within a short timespan, I was able to fully develop functional models. General Motors Hybrid Control Engineering Intern September 2014 - February 2015 (6 months) Canadian Regional Engineering Centre As an intern in the Hybrid Control Engineering team, my main project was to integrate the new cooling pump with the existing control system architecture of the GM cars.

As a result, I developed Simulink control models and diagnostic strategies that complied with the corporate best practices. I evaluated the models using the test benches as well as test vehicles. Furthermore, I developed the necessary functional requirements documentation and test cases to troubleshoot issues within the systems. This experience taught me how to work effectively as a member of a large international organization. The Centre for Applied Power Electronics (CAPE) May 2012 - February 2014 (1 year 10 months) My first research experience as an undergraduate was at the Centre for Applied Power Electronics (CAPE) at the University of Toronto under the supervision of Professor Reza Iravani, where I have worked for two years, partially funded by an NSERC-USRA award. (57) ABSTRACT

Intravascular imaging catheters are provided that include a distal sheath portion having a lumen that is configured to optionally receive a guidewire or an imaging assembly. The distal sheath portion may be configured to have dimensions such that when a guidewire is inserted through the lumen and extends through a distal exit port, the distal sheath portion may be employed as a microcatheter. External tissue may be imaged at a location at or near the distal end of the catheter, enabling, for example, the controlled imaging of a total occlusion, and the positioning of the distal end (and guidewire) within a true lumen associated with a total occlusion. A structural stop may be provided at or near the distal end of the distal sheath portion to prohibit extension of the imaging assembly out of the distal exit port, while permitting the extension of the guidewire through the distal exit port.

## BACKGROUND

The present disclosure relates to medical imaging. More particularly, the present disclosure relates to intravascular imaging in the presence of a total occlusion.

Intravascular imaging, such as intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) and optical coherence tomography (OCT), are frequently used for the assessment of coronary artery disease to supplement angiographic images. Most uses of intravascular imaging occur in vessels that are not occluded and therefore have some residual lumen through which angiographic dye, guidewires and imaging catheters can be delivered. Intravascular imaging is also occasionally used for interventions on total occlusions (such as chronic total occlusions). However, this typically occurs either after the total occlusion has been dilated open with an angioplasty balloon or after stent placement. In this context, the intravascular images provide an assessment of the results of the procedure.

r after the total occlusion has been dilated open with an angioplasty balloon or after stent placement. In this context, the intravascular images provide an assessment of the results of the procedure.

Chronic total occlusions (CTOs) are particularly challenging lesions in which to perform interventional procedures, as they are difficult to visualize angiographically due to the absence of a lumen in which dye resides. The absence ofa lumen also means that the routine techniques of advancing guidewires and other equipment that rely on a patent jumen surrounded by an arterial wall that confines the devices to remain intraluminal to direct the equipment are not as readily applied. They are also difficult to cross and often require the use of more aggressive and potentially traumatic devices, such as guidewires with stiff tips and devices that penetrate into the subintimal layers of the vessel and then re-emerge into the true lumen (also known as subintimal dissection and re-entry).

The use of IVUS or OCT to guide the treatment of CTOs is increasing and early data suggests it may improve procedural outcomes.

## SUMMARY

Intravascular imaging catheters are provided that include a distal sheath portion having a lumen that is configured to optionally receive a guidewire or an imaging assembly. The distal sheath portion may be configured to have dimensions such that when a guidewire is inserted through the lumen and extends through a distal exit port, the distal sheath portion may be employed as a microcatheter. External tissue may be imaged at a location at or near the distal end of the catheter, enabling, for example, the controlled imaging of a total occlusion, and the positioning of the distal end (and guidewire) within a true lumen associated with a total occlusion. A structural stop may be provided at or near the distal end of the distal sheath portion to prohibit extension

of the imaging assembly out of the distal exit port, while permitting the extension of the guidewire through the distal exit port.

Accordingly, in a first aspect, there is provided an intravascular imaging catheter comprising:

a proximal elongate sheath comprising a first lumen;

an imaging conduit having a proximal portion housed within the first lumen and a distal portion extending beyond. a distal end of said proximal elongate sheath;

an imaging assembly connected to said imaging conduit at a location that is remote from a proximal end thereof; and

al elongate sheath segment;

an imaging conduit retractable within the first himen; and

an imaging assembly connected to said imaging conduit at a location that is remote from a proximal end thereof;

wherein said imaging conduit is extendable within the third lumen when the third lumen is absent of the guidewire, such that said imaging assembly is extendable within the third lumen to a distal region of said distal elongate sheath segment, without extending beyond said distal end of said distal elongate sheath segment.

A further understanding of the functional and advantageous aspects of the disclosure can be realized by reference to the following detailed description and drawings.

As used herein, the phrase "microcatheter" refers to a catheter having a diameter, at least within a distal region thereof, that is less than 4 French (F) in diameter and typically less than or equal to 3 F in diameter. A microcatheter may have features that aid its deliverability through occlusions and into the distal vasculature, such as a tapered soft tip, wire braided reinforcement of the wall over all or a portion of its length, a hydrophilic coating on its outer surface and a lubricious lining (such as a PTFE liner) on the inner surface of the wall. There may also be a marker band, such as a gold, platinum or other radio-opaque material marker band in the distal tip to help it be readily identifiable on fluoroscopy. A microcatheter may have a single lumen or a plurality of lumens.

One of the limitations of most IVUS and OCT catheters is that they are side-viewing in nature, and collect images by rotating an ultrasound beam or optical beam around the long axis of the catheter. Usually, the beam has an angle of 80 to 90 degrees relative to the longitudinal axis of the catheter, allowing the creation of cross-sectional images. In some embodiments disclosed in the art (Novelis, others) the beam is tilted in a more forward viewing direction.

e longitudinal axis of the catheter, allowing the creation of cross-sectional images. In some embodiments disclosed in the art (Novelis, others) the beam is tilted in a more forward viewing direction.

Intravascular imaging catheters typically have a guidewire lumen near the distal end in what is referred to as a rapid-exchange configuration. In this configuration, the distal portion of the catheter (say 5 to 20 mm) is configured to surround a guidewire that exits the guidewire lumen at the distal end of the catheter via a distal guidewire port. A more proximal portion of the guidewire exits the catheter's guidewire lumen via a proximal guidewire port that is typically 5 to 20 mm proximal to the distal guidewire port.

Due to the limited diameter of the intravascular imaging catheter (constrained by the size limitations imposed by the diameter of vessels such as coronary arteries), the imaging assembly from which the ultrasound or imaging beam radiates from the catheter is typically offset proximal to the proximal guidewire port. Thus, the distance between the tip of an intravascular imaging catheter and the region imaged by the intravascular imaging catheter is typically greater than the length of the guidewire lumen.

e port. Thus, the distance between the tip of an intravascular imaging catheter and the region imaged by the intravascular imaging catheter is typically greater than the length of the guidewire lumen.

In many cases in intravascular imaging, it is desirable to have as short as possible of a distance between the tip of the intravascular imaging catheter and the region from within the catheter at which the imaging beam exits the catheter. However, with a conventional intravascular imaging catheter, the technique used is to first advance a guidewire far enough into the vasculature such that an intravascular imaging probe can then be advanced over the guidewire to get to the target space. The guidewire preferably should always extend beyond the distal tip of the intravascular imaging catheter to minimize the likelihood of trauma to the anatomy from the distal tip of the intravascular imaging catheter. This means that, at a minimum, with conventional intravascular imaging catheters, the closest that an imaging transducer

could get to a blunt occlusion in the cardiovascular anatomy without having the guidewire penetrate into the occlusion is the sum of i) a short length of guidewire extending out past the distal tip of the intravascular imaging catheter, and ii) the distance between the distal tip of the intravascular imaging catheter and the imaging assembly within the intravascular imaging catheter.

distal tip of the intravascular imaging catheter, and ii) the distance between the distal tip of the intravascular imaging catheter and the imaging assembly within the intravascular imaging catheter.

The problem posed by this limitation is further exacerbated by the fact that most intravascular imaging catheters are delivered over guidewires that have a stiff long portion, followed by a more floppy tip. The floppy tip of the guidewire is intended to increase deliverability of the guidewire through non-occluded vessels while minimizing trauma during its delivery. The floppy tip is conventionally 35 to 45 mm long, but other lengths are available and there is a great deal of variability in guidewire design. If the distal tip of the intravascular imaging catheter is advanced onto the floppy tip of the guidewire, it is more likely that upon retraction (withdrawal) of the intravascular imaging catheter over the guidewire, that the floppy tip of the guidewire will buckle, thus damaging the guidewire, the intravascular imaging catheter and/or the anatomy through which the surrounding anatomy. The ability to advance intravascular image catheters in distal vasculature, in small vessels and near occlusions is limited in part by the ability to bring the imaging portion of the intravascular imaging catheter into a position near an anatomic region of interest during the procedure such that the desired views can be obtained.

by the ability to bring the imaging portion of the intravascular imaging catheter into a position near an anatomic region of interest during the procedure such that the desired views can be obtained.

Furthermore, it is desirable to have configurations of intravascular imaging catheters with as low a profile as possible to minimize trauma during insertion of the imaging catheter (such as if the catheter unintentionally exits the lumen of the artery and puts the patient at risk for a coronary perforation) and to minimize compression of the true lumen such that it is more easily recognizable by intravascular imaging.

Terumo has introduced an IVUS catheter that has a very short distance between the tip of the IVUS catheter and the ultrasound transducer, called the Navifocus WR. Its design minimizes the distance between the transducer and the catheter tip, but does not address the fact that a significant length of guidewire needs to be advanced ahead of the catheter. However, the monorail guidewire lumen runs parallel to the imaging lumen over most of its distal length and thus the maximum cross-sectional dimension for the distal portion of this catheter is larger (2.4 F or 0.8 mm for the imaging lumen and another approximately 1.5 F or 0.5 mm for the guidewire lumen, leading to a total maximum dimension of approximately 1.3 mm).

tion of this catheter is larger (2.4 F or 0.8 mm for the imaging lumen and another approximately 1.5 F or 0.5 mm for the guidewire lumen, leading to a total maximum dimension of approximately 1.3 mm).

Referring back to how a chronic total occlusion is treated, it will become apparent that the distance between the tip of the imaging catheter and the points at which imaging energy exits the imaging catheter (and thus generates images) is a limitation of the utility of intravascular imaging during CTO revascularization procedures.

Typically, the CTO is crossed using one or more guidewires. Softer guidewires are less traumatic, but may not be strong enough to penetrate through fibrous cap or calcified regions in coronary occlusions. Stiffer wires have more directional control and pushability, but may cause more trauma to the vessel and surrounding anatomy, such as by causing dissections or perforations to the vessel.

Causing trauma with a wire such as a dissection or perforation can have variable consequences, depending on the location of the trauma and the physiologic significance of the trauma. For example, advancing a wire in the subintima of a vessel that is completely occluded is less likely to

uences, depending on the location of the trauma and the physiologic significance of the trauma. For example, advancing a wire in the subintima of a vessel that is completely occluded is less likely to

cause a physiologically meaningful dissection flap or ischemia, as the vessel is already occluded and therefore the layers of the vessel wall push against each other with little neighboring blood flow. Therefore the creation of a dissection in a CTO is unlikely to abruptly deprive a significant region of myocardium of blood. In fact, many CTO operators advance devices such as angioplasty balloons, blunt dissection devices and re-entry devices into the subintimal space of a CTO to work towards creating a new lumen across the length of the CTO. However, it is highly desirable to create that new lumen within the adventitial boundaries of the occluded vessel to ensure that there is some structural support for the new lumen that might otherwise result in loss of the gross architecture of the new lumen and potentially result in a perforation. Perforation of a coronary vessel into the pericardial space can result in fluid accumulating into the pericardial space causing a condition called tamponade, which is potentially acutely life threatening.

oration of a coronary vessel into the pericardial space can result in fluid accumulating into the pericardial space causing a condition called tamponade, which is potentially acutely life threatening.

Extending a dissection plane significantly beyond the distal cap of a CTO can also have clinical consequences, as the distal vasculature may be fed by collateral branches and involved in the perfusion of functioning myocardium. Creating a dissection in the distal vascular can compromise the perfusion and thus potentially cause ischemia or infarction.

It should be mentioned that while perforation with a small wire can be dangerous in some situations, it is the advancement, dilatation and deployment of balloons and stents over a guidewire that can introduce the most risk during a CTO procedure if the guidewire does not reside within the true lumen of the occluded vessel, where a perforation or dissection can be expanded in size, thus increasing the severity of a perforation or the extent of a region effected by a dissection. The advancement of a wire into a false lumen or into the pericardial space is much more likely to be a recoverable situation if balloon dilatation has not yet taken place.

on effected by a dissection. The advancement of a wire into a false lumen or into the pericardial space is much more likely to be a recoverable situation if balloon dilatation has not yet taken place.

In some situations where intravascular imaging is used to facilitate CTO procedures, the imaging catheter may be placed in an adjacent vessel or in a branch of the same vessel to help with guiding the penetration of the fibrous cap of a CTO. IVUS is perhaps more useful for this as, unlike most optical methods, IVUS can see through blood and provide real-time imaging without the need to transiently displace blood from the field of view. This act of penetrating the fibrous cap or entry point into the CTO is a frequently challenging step that may require the use of a stiff guidewire and is frustrated in many cases by the absence of angiographic clues that help the operator identify the entry point into the CTO. Intravascular imaging can help identify the entry point and provide visual guidance/confirmation during the advancement of the wire if the imaging portion of the catheter can be advanced into a position and orientation close enough to the entry point that places the proximal portion of the cap into the field of view of the imaging catheter.

ging portion of the catheter can be advanced into a position and orientation close enough to the entry point that places the proximal portion of the cap into the field of view of the imaging catheter.

Another situation in which an imaging catheter can be useful during a CTO procedure is to confirm that the wire resides within at least the adventitial contour of the vessel, and preferably in the true lumen of the vessel. Advancing an intravascular imaging catheter over the guidewire and into the CTO may cause some trauma to the vessel, but is generally less traumatic than deploying a balloon or stent in the wrong location. If the adventitial and/or intimal layers of the vessel can be identified as surrounding the intravascular imaging catheter, this generally provides some reassurance that the guidewire is within the confines of the vessel over

at least the portions of the CTO that can be imaged with the intravascular imaging catheter.

ter, this generally provides some reassurance that the guidewire is within the confines of the vessel over

at least the portions of the CTO that can be imaged with the intravascular imaging catheter.

Alternatively, if the intravascular imaging catheter (and thus the guidewire over which it was delivered) is found to have exited the true lumen or the adventitial contours, one or more additional guidewires might be advanced into the CTO to try and create a preferred path through the CTO. It may then be possible to visualize the position of additional guidewires during their manipulation by the operator while the intravascular imaging catheter is in place and thus be possible to achieve a preferred path through the CTO more easily. It should be mentioned that once the imaging portion of an intravascular imaging catheter has exited a patent lumen and has entered into a CTO, there is far less blood in the field of view and it may be easier to use optical imaging within the CTO as compared to in a lumen where there is blood.

xited a patent lumen and has entered into a CTO, there is far less blood in the field of view and it may be easier to use optical imaging within the CTO as compared to in a lumen where there is blood.

Another situation in which an intravascular imaging catheter is potentially useful for CTO procedures is in confirming whether or not the guidewire has successfully entered into the true lumen of the distal vasculature. It is occasionally difficult to confirm angiographically whether or not guidewire has advanced through the lesion and exited into the true lumen of the distal vasculature. It is preferable to ensure that the wire (and subsequently the balloons and stents) are in the true lumen rather than within the layers of the wall, as continuing along in such a procedure without having confidence that the distal end of the wire is in the true lumen can greatly complicate the procedure.

rather than within the layers of the wall, as continuing along in such a procedure without having confidence that the distal end of the wire is in the true lumen can greatly complicate the procedure.

In CTO interventions, the use of microcatheters in combination with guidewires is common. Microcatheters are typically thin catheters with one or more lumens large enough to accommodate a guidewire. Many microcatheters are 1.8 F in diameter and can accommodate a 0.014" coronary guidewire, but other sizes are available to accommodate coronary and peripheral interventions. Examples of microcatheters in use include the FineCross catheter, the Supercross, the Corsair, the Valet Microcatheter and others. The use of a microcatheters may enhance the torquability and deliverability of a guidewire through portions of the coronary anatomy that are calcified, tortuous or severely stenotic/occluded by providing a better interface with the surface of the wire. Furthermore, microcatheters play an important role in terms of facilitating the use of different guidewire configurations at different times during an attempt to traverse an occlusion. For example, it may be desirable to advance a particular guidewire with a preferred bend to the entry point of a CTO. For example, this may be a soft tip wire (such as a BMW or Whisper wire) with a modest bend at the tip to help navigate the proximal portion of the coronary vasculature.

d to the entry point of a CTO. For example, this may be a soft tip wire (such as a BMW or Whisper wire) with a modest bend at the tip to help navigate the proximal portion of the coronary vasculature.

Once the guidewire is at the entry point, it may be preferable to use a guidewire that has a much stiffer tip, such as a Confianza wire or Miracle Bros wire with a very short bend at the tip. In order to switch from one wire type to another, a microcatheter can be advanced over the first wire. The first wire can then be withdrawn from the vasculature through the microcatheter. Then, the next wire can be advanced through the microcatheter. This technique helps provide a reliable and less traumatic path for the second wire to get to approximately the same position as where the first wire was previously advanced. The microcatheter may have a relatively atraumatic tip, depending on its design. Then, once the second wire is advanced through the proximal entry point of the CTO, the second wire can be advanced some distance through the CTO.

a relatively atraumatic tip, depending on its design. Then, once the second wire is advanced through the proximal entry point of the CTO, the second wire can be advanced some distance through the CTO.

At some point (either within the CTO, upon approach to the distal exit point or even upon reaching the distal vasculature), it may be desirable to then exchange the second wire out for yet another wire, such as a wire with a less traumatic tip, a wire specifically designed to carry a rotational atherectomy device or some other wire. The microcatheter can be advanced at any time over the wire to either help facilitate delivery of the wire or to enable the exchange of one wire for another. Once a guidewire has successfully reached the distal vasculature, the use of balloons, stents and other interventional devices to revascularize the CTO can be used to get the desired result of revascularizing the CTO.

At any point during the performance of a CTO procedure or other interventional procedures, it may be desirable to perform imaging from within a microcatheter, and in particular, near the distal tip of a microcatheter.

A first example embodiment of the disclosure is provided in FIG, 1. An example intravascular imaging catheter 100 includes a separable outer sheath with a distal elongate sheath portion 1 and a proximal elongate sheath portion 4, where the proximal 4 and distal sheath portions 1 of the separable outer sheath are removably connectable via distal sheath connector 2 and proximal sheath connector 3. In one example implementation, connectors 2 and 3 may be mating Luer connectors, as is commonly used in interventional catheters, or, for example, magnetic connectors, snap connectors, screw-threaded connectors or other connectors that provide a mechanical connection and preferably a watertight connection.

The proximal sheath portion 4 may further include a connector 8 that connects the intravascular imaging catheter to a patient interface module (alternatively called a motor drive unit). In one example implementation, connector 8 may include a latch or release button 6 that latches the proximal connector 8 to a motor drive unit.

The outer sheath contains a main lumen 11, formed by a first lumen that extends through the proximal sheath portion 4 and a second lumen that extends through the distal sheath portion 1. The first and second lumens are brought into fluid communication when the two sheath portions are connected.

ximal sheath portion 4 and a second lumen that extends through the distal sheath portion 1. The first and second lumens are brought into fluid communication when the two sheath portions are connected.

As described below, the main lumen 11 has a diameter suitable for the insertion of an imaging assembly 10 via a longitudinal shaft or cable (e.g. a torque cable for rotating the imaging assembly within the distal sheath portion 1), henceforth referred to as an imaging conduit. The imaging assembly is connected to the imaging conduit at a location that is remote from a proximal end of the imaging conduit.

For example, FIG. 1 shows imaging assembly 10 located near a distal end of distal sheath portion 1, where imaging assembly 10 is rotatable within main lumen 11, for example, via rotation of the imaging conduit (not shown in FIG. 1) that extends within main lumen 11 from the proximal connector 6 into the distal sheath portion 1. During rotation, imaging beams 12 can be transmitted into, and received from, the local external environment in order to obtain signals for generating an image within the field of view 9. In one example embodiment, the imaging beams 12 may be ultrasound beams. In another example embodiment, the imaging beams 12 may be optical beams. The imaging beams 12 may corresponding to a single imaging modality, or a plurality of imaging modalities.

e ultrasound beams. In another example embodiment, the imaging beams 12 may be optical beams. The imaging beams 12 may corresponding to a single imaging modality, or a plurality of imaging modalities.

As shown in FIG. 1, a flush port 7 may optionally be attached to or integrated with the proximal sheath portion 4 (or some other portion of the proximal portion of the catheter), where the flush port 7 provides a port through which fluid can be introduced into the main lumen 11, for example, in order to flush the catheter of air.

As shown in FIG. 1, the proximal sheath portion 4 may also have two separate sheath components (4 and 5) that slide longitudinally with respect to each other to enable longitudinal translation (e.g. pullback) of the imaging assembly 10 within the intravascular imaging catheter 100.

As shown in FIG. 1, the main lumen 11 of the intravascular imaging catheter is sufficiently large enough to accommodate the insertion and internal translation (and optional rotation) of the imaging assembly 10 and an associated imaging conduit (such as a torque cable and either some electrical conductors or one or more fiber optics). As will be described in further detail below, in some embodiments, a distal region of main lumen 11, within the distal sheath portion 1, may be sufficiently narrow to prohibit the imaging assembly from extending beyond the distal end of the distal sheath portion 1.

The second lumen of the distal sheath portion is also sufficiently large to accommodate a guidewire in the absence of the insertion of the imaging assembly 10 and the imaging conduit. As will be known to those skilled in the art, guidewires range in size, however, in coronary interventions, guidewires are typically approximately 0.014" in diameter, and typically range from 0.009" to 0.018" in diameter, with guidewires typically being 0.014" to 0.038" for peripheral interventions.

The distal sheath portion 1 is long enough to extend through a guide catheter (not shown) and into the vasculature, where the guide catheter is separate from catheter 13, such that catheter 13 can be inserted into the guide catheter. For coronary applications, guide catheters are typically 90 to 100 cm long. As such, in coronary applications, the distal sheath portion of catheter 13 is typically more than 90 cm long and more typically 120-160 cm long, such that its distal end can be extended beyond the distal end of the guide catheter.
