For example, unlike most microcatheters that have metal reinforcement up to near their distal end, the distal sheath portion 1 should be transmissive to the imaging energy that is used during imaging, for at least those sub-portions of the distal portion in which imaging is to take place. For example, it may be desirable to allow imaging only with the imaging assembly 10 is at its distalmost position within the main lumen. Alternatively, there may be a distal sub-portion, (e.g. a distal imaging window), such as a 10 cm long portion of the distal region of the distal sheath portion 1 that is transmissive to the imaging media to enable imaging during

pullback. As such, the material forming the distal sheath portion 1 should be selected to permit this imaging.

In general, a high density of metal braiding precludes imaging with ultrasound or optical imaging. Typical materials with the desired properties for either optical or acoustic imaging include HDPE, LDPE, Pebax, Nylon, TPX and other polymer known in the art. The actual grade of polymer within each family can be selected based on the mechanical properties desired and the ability to mechanically couple the imaging window to other portions of the catheter (such as by thermal bonding, adhesives, crimping and other methods.

sed on the mechanical properties desired and the ability to mechanically couple the imaging window to other portions of the catheter (such as by thermal bonding, adhesives, crimping and other methods.

In various applications, it may be important or beneficial to position the imaging assembly 10 accurately within the distal region of the distal sheath portion 1. For example, it may be desirable to position the imaging assembly 10 to a distalmost location within the main lumen 11. However, this may be challenging to achieve when the imaging conduit includes a torque cable. A torque cable is typically hollow and may contain electrical wires or one or more fiber optics that transmit imaging signals between the imaging assembly 10 and proximal connector 8. The imaging conduit (e.g. a torque cable) may include one or more layers of wound. metal filaments around a hollow lumen. The torque cable is preferably quite flexible, yet has reliable one-to-one transmission of torque between its proximal and distal ends. Torque cables may be quite compressible or extensible along their longitudinal axis, depending on how they are constructed.

able one-to-one transmission of torque between its proximal and distal ends. Torque cables may be quite compressible or extensible along their longitudinal axis, depending on how they are constructed.

One challenge with a torque cable that can stretch or compress along its long axis that relates to the present disclosure is that advancement of the torque cable within the distal sheath portion 1 may result in a suboptimal distance between the distal tip of the catheter and the imaging assembly. During advancement through the main lumen 11, the imaging conduit 17 would likely be longitudinally compressed due to friction between both the inner surface of the distal sheath portion 1 and the outer surfaces of the imaging assembly 10 and the imaging conduit 17. If the torque cable is then rotated, the imaging conduit 17 may or may not be relieved of some of that compression. In other words, if it is assumed that the imaging conduit is of fixed length, then the presence of friction and compression may cause the actual location of the imaging assembly within the distal sheath portion 1 to be proximal to the estimated position.

Furthermore, in some cases, the imaging assembly 10 may unintentionally extend out the distal port 14 of the intravascular imaging catheter, thus exposing the anatomy to rotating components and potentially inflicting trauma to the vessel and/or causing the device to fail. On other hand, if the imaging conduit is too short, the imaging assembly 10 will not be as distal as it otherwise could be.

Dear Hiring Manager,

Please accept this letter along with the enclosed resume as my application for the Applied Research Intern position at Intuitive Surgical. I am currently a second year master student in Clinical Engineering program at the Institute of Biomaterials and Biomedical Engineering at the University of Toronto. I am intrigued by the world-class innovative technologies being developed at Intuitive Surgical, and being a part of an organization dedicated to innovation and pushing the boundaries of the standards of care is truly a dream come true. Given the opportunity, I strongly believe I would be able to make a positive contribution in implementing technologies that impact human lives with the objective of improving healthcare quality.

true. Given the opportunity, I strongly believe I would be able to make a positive contribution in implementing technologies that impact human lives with the objective of improving healthcare quality.

My master's thesis is focused on developing an intraprocedural navigation workflow for radiofrequency ablation (RFA) used in the treatment of spinal metastases. The goal of my research is to integrate RFA treatment into existing radiation and image-guided therapy pathways for localized vertebral cancer treatment, as a result I have been collaborating with a multidisciplinary team of clinicians, medical physicists, and engineers. Thus far, I have developed a navigation application for vertebral RFA as a scripted loadable Python extension within the 3D Slicer platform that integrates RFA treatment plans based on pre-procedural CT imaging data with intra-procedural 3D cone beam CT images. The next steps of the project are to evaluate the accuracy and reproducibility of the workflow by conducting phantom studies followed by animal studies. Furthermore, I have formally presented my work in various settings such as a student seminar, committee meeting, to industry collaborators, and conferences. I believe that the knowledge and skills I have gained through my research project will directly translate to making a positive and noteworthy contribution at Intuitive Surgical.

My undergraduate work in the medical field involved developing methodologies to design and build various cardiac catheters, each designed for a specific use within minimally invasive cardiac interventions. During this time, I learnt about the development and validation process of miniature medical devices for minimally-invasive treatment and image guidance for cardiovascular diseases. This industry-collaborative research work included conducting literature reviews, outlining the project-specific constraints and requirements, creating prototypes of catheters, developing and conducting experiments to optimize design parameters, and coordinating purchases of materials. My most notable achievement during this term was developing a new method (patent pending) for assembling intra-cardiac catheters. To achieve my objectives, I used Matlab and SolidWorks software for design parameter tuning and 3D modeling, as well as hardware such as YAG laser, lathes, horizontal traverse, and braiding machine. Introduction The first aim of the project is to develop a modular and gamified robotic rehabilitation system designed to enhance engagement and motivation in stroke patients with upper limb impairments. The system integrates advanced hardware and software components to deliver personalized and adaptive rehabilitation exercises, supporting posture and balance, weight-bearing activities, and range of motion (ROM) training, with a design that allows for both individual and team-based activities. Key features include adjustable and secure orthotic shells, controlled assistive/resistive arm movements through single actuator unit and multi-modal sensors, and real-time feedback mechanisms. The system's gamified interface provides engaging and adaptive exercises, leveraging AI-based algorithms to adjust difficulty levels based on patient performance. By facilitating independent use and providing detailed performance tracking, the robotic system aims to improve patient engagement, adherence to rehabilitation programs, and overall recovery outcomes. In addition, by fostering a sense of community through multiplayer modes, the system addresses patient isolation and aims to further improve adherence to rehabilitation programs. This document encompasses the design requirements and detailed system specifications necessary for developing a modular and gamified robotic rehabilitation system for stroke patients with upper limb impairment. It includes requirements for the mechanical, electrical, and software components, ensuring all functional, performance, and safety aspects are addressed.

Detailed system specifications cover the system architecture, hardware and software components, control algorithms, user interfaces, and data processing methods, outlining the technical details necessary for implementation and integration. Additionally, the document outlines the progress made so far in the design and development of various aspects of the system. The document also provides details on the future steps as well as the milestones for the remainder of the project. Objectives and goals: Primary Objectives: 1. Develop a Modular and Adaptable Robotic System: o Create a cost-effective, modular robotic rehabilitation platform that can be easily customized to fit the individual needs of stroke patients with upper limb impairments. o As patients needs change over the course of their rehabilitation, the system adapts and provides customized assistance o As patients improve, some of the changes to the system include ▪ From static to dynamic weight bearing exercises ▪ Increase the repetitions and duration of balance and posture exercises ▪ Increase the ROM limits ▪ Assist-as-needed > resistive forces during ROM exercises 2. Enhance Patient Engagement and Motivation: o Integrate adaptive, gamified exercises to maintain high levels of patient motivation and engagement throughout the rehabilitation process.

ROM exercises 2. Enhance Patient Engagement and Motivation: o Integrate adaptive, gamified exercises to maintain high levels of patient motivation and engagement throughout the rehabilitation process. o Keep patients at the success rate of 70-90% by making the exercises dynamically easier or harder based on their performance o As patients get better, we expect the following changes ▪ Increase repetitions ▪ Decrease assistive torque ▪ Increase ROM limits ▪ Increase speed ▪ Increase accuracy ▪ Increase resistive torque 3. Support Various Rehabilitation Exercises: o Provide a range of exercises including weight-bearing activities, posture and balance, and range of motion (ROM) exercises. o This provides a complete workout routine per session, which is a representative of rehabilitation session. 4. Facilitate Independent Use: o Design the system for independent use by patients, minimizing the need for constant supervision and allowing for home-based rehabilitation. o Aimed to have most of the system put together with a single hand 5. Build a Supportive Community: o Incorporate multiplayer modes (cooperative and competitive) to foster a sense of community and peer support, enhancing patient motivation and adherence to rehabilitation programs. 6. Provide Real-Time Feedback and Performance Tracking: o Implement real-time feedback mechanisms and performance tracking to guide patients during exercises and provide valuable data to therapists for optimizing rehabilitation plans.

Expected Outcomes: • Improved Patient Compliance: o Higher rates of adherence to prescribed rehabilitation exercises due to increased engagement and motivation from gamified elements and community support. • Enhanced Rehabilitation Outcomes: o Significant improvements in motor function, strength, and overall recovery of upper limb capabilities in stroke patients. • Reduced Burden on Therapists: o Decreased need for continuous therapist supervision, allowing therapists to focus on personalized care and strategic planning. • Scalable and Accessible Rehabilitation: o A scalable solution that can be implemented in various settings, including clinics and homes, making high-quality rehabilitation accessible to a broader population. • Robust Data Collection for Clinical Insights: o Comprehensive data on patient performance and progress, enabling more informed clinical decisions and personalized therapy adjustments.

Background Stroke Stroke is the leading global cause of disability, primarily due to disruptions in brain cell perfusion that result in cell death and connectivity loss. This condition often leads to upper limb impairments, especially when the middle cerebral artery infarction affects the primary motor cortex and corticospinal tract integrity. Such impairments manifest as weakness, reduced interjoint coordination, and diminished finger dexterity. The pathological flexor synergy, characterized by stereotypical co-activation of elbow flexion and shoulder abduction, and compensatory trunk movements in reaching tasks, are notable motor performance deficits. These movement abnormalities may occur in isolation or combination, vary in chronic manifestation based on the deficit's severity and the affected cerebral region, posing ongoing challenges in rehabilitation [1]. Capturing upper limb movement characteristics is crucial for enhancing understanding of stroke-related movement deficits and their underlying dysfunctions, and for evaluating treatment approaches. Assessment methods range from qualitative descriptions in clinical records to observer-based clinical scales and detailed kinematic motion analysis.

Clinical tools like the FuglMeyer Assessment and the Action Research Arm Test, while reliable, often lack the detail needed to distinguish between physiological and pathological movements. Kinematic assessments offer detailed, objective insights into movement quality, revealing issues like prolonged movement times and greater trunk displacement. However, their integration into clinical practice is limited by the diversity of measurement systems and the need for expensive, fixed laboratory setups [1]. Progress Rehabilitation Program Despite the absence of precisely defined dose-response relationships, it is evident that recovery is influenced by the volume of movement practice. Supervised one-on-one therapy sessions often do not provide a sufficient volume of exercise to meet therapeutic requirements. Consequently, home exercise programs are recommended to augment the volume of movement training [2]. These exercises are designed to be easily performed in a home setting, promoting consistent practice and facilitating motor recovery. Examples include towel slides, bottle lifting, supported reaching and grasping, modified push-ups, and mirror therapy [1], [3], [4], [5]. Drawing upon a comprehensive review of prescribed home-based exercises, direct observation of clinicians during outpatient rehabilitation sessions, and in-depth interviews with healthcare professionals, I have developed a structured daily regimen aimed at enhancing upper limb function in stroke patients.

This regimen encompasses warm-up activities targeting the upper body, muscle activation exercises for the affected regions, and range of motion (ROM) exercises designed to improve functional movements essential for daily tasks, such as reaching. The system incorporates three primary exercise categories: posture and balance, weight-bearing, and ROM exercises. Posture and balance exercises serve to prepare the upper body, static and dynamic weight-bearing activities stimulate neural pathways, and ROM exercises focus on enhancing arm mobility. To promote patient engagement and adherence to the prescribed home routine, it is imperative to design a

earing activities stimulate neural pathways, and ROM exercises focus on enhancing arm mobility. To promote patient engagement and adherence to the prescribed home routine, it is imperative to design a

modular, gamified robotic system that seamlessly integrates these exercises, thereby fostering motivation and facilitating sustained participation in the rehabilitation process. Below a detailed breakdown of each exercise category is provided. Types of Exercises Supported 1. Posture and Balance Exercises: o Deep Breaths: Encourages diaphragmatic breathing to improve respiratory function and relaxation. o Back Extensor - Isometric Hold: Strengthens the muscles of the back through static holds. o Trunk Extension/Flexion: Enhances flexibility and strength of the trunk muscles. o Trunk Circles: Improves trunk mobility and stability through circular movements.
2. Weight Bearing Exercises: o Supports different levels and ranges of weight-bearing exercises at the palm of the hand or on the forearm to enhance strength and stability.
3. Range of Motion (ROM) Exercises: o Shoulder (zero angle is when arm is down straight): ▪ Abduction/Adduction: 0 to 90 degrees w.r.t the y-axis, rotation about z-axis. It can go past 90 degrees to 180 degrees, but it depends on the stage in rehab and presence of shoulder joint complications. ▪ Flexion/Extension: 0 to 90 degrees w.r.t the y-axis, rotation about xaxis. It can go from -40 to 90 degrees realistically, but 0 to 90 degrees is a good start, above 90 can be done if no joint complications at advance stages. ▪ Rotation: -80 to 80 degrees w.r.t the z-axis, rotation about y-axis. o Elbow: ▪ Flexion/Extension: 0 to about 150/160 degrees w.r.t the y-axis, rotation about x-axis. o Wrist: ▪ Pronation/Supination: Thumb pointing up is 0 degrees, pronation is -90 degrees, supination is around 70-80 degrees.

sion: 0 to about 150/160 degrees w.r.t the y-axis, rotation about x-axis. o Wrist: ▪ Pronation/Supination: Thumb pointing up is 0 degrees, pronation is -90 degrees, supination is around 70-80 degrees.

Figure 1The axis definition with respect to human body Compensatory Movements In the context of prescribed home-based rehabilitation exercises for stroke patients, it is crucial to monitor and address compensatory movements that may impede recovery and lead to maladaptive patterns. Based on selected exercises and consultations with expert clinicians, the following compensatory movements have been identified as critical to monitor and correct: 1. Slouching or Hunching Over: Patients may exhibit a tendency to slouch or hunch instead of maintaining an upright seated posture.
2. Leaning to One Side: A common compensatory behavior involves leaning toward the impaired side, which can affect balance and proper muscle engagement.
3. Elevating the Shoulder During ROM Exercises: Instead of isolating arm movements, patients might raise the shoulder, leading to improper movement mechanics.
4. Trunk Flexion Instead of Arm Extension: During reaching tasks or various ROM exercises, patients may move their trunk forward rather than extending the arm, reducing the effectiveness of the exercise.
5. Collapsing into the Seat: Failing to maintain an upright posture, patients might "melt" into the seat, diminishing core engagement and postural control. To effectively detect and provide feedback on these compensatory movements, the integration of video analysis, inertial measurement unit (IMU) data, and providing audiovisual as well as haptic cues are proposed. Progress on the technical aspects is addressed in the later sections of this report. This approach aims to offer real-time corrective cues, enabling patients to adjust their movements and adhere to optimal motor patterns during rehabilitation exercises. Addressing

ions of this report. This approach aims to offer real-time corrective cues, enabling patients to adjust their movements and adhere to optimal motor patterns during rehabilitation exercises. Addressing

these compensatory strategies is essential, as reliance on such movements can hinder the rehabilitation process and potentially lead to secondary complications. Specific Exercises: Here are further compensatory movements to avoid for specific exercises. Shoulder Rotation: Elbow Close to Body: Keep the elbow as close to the body as possible to perform true shoulder rotation. Shoulder Abduction and Flexion: Elbow Straight: Maintain a straight elbow during shoulder abduction and flexion exercises. Additional Considerations: Gradual Increase: If compensations are observed, reduce the range of motion to what can be performed correctly and gradually increase it. High-level system architecture: This section describes the overall system, showing major components and their interactions.
## 1. User Interface Layer • User Interface (UI): Provides an interactive interface for patients to engage with the system. o Displays: Screen or VR headset for visual feedback. o Input Devices: Buttons, keyboard, microphone, or other input methods for user interaction.
• Real-Time Feedback: to encourage users to correct their posture and/or maintain engagement throughout the session. o Haptic Feedback: piezoelectric actuators at certain location on the upper body provide user with the queuing necessary to initiate or correct movement. o Visual Feedback: Provides the user with visual cues on a screen or VR headset, helping to guide the user through exercises. o Auditory Feedback: Provides motivational prompts and corrective feedback to enhance the user's experience.
## 2. Application Layer • Game Engine: Manages the gamified rehabilitation exercises, including rendering graphics, controlling game flow, and adapting game difficulty. o Game Logic: Contains rules and mechanics of rehabilitation games.

e: Manages the gamified rehabilitation exercises, including rendering graphics, controlling game flow, and adapting game difficulty. o Game Logic: Contains rules and mechanics of rehabilitation games.

• AI-based Adaptive Difficulty: manages the rehab routine based on the personal user data using contextual bandits, RL, or other AI methods.
• Real-Time Feedback: Provides immediate haptic, visual, and auditory feedback to the user.
## 3. Control Layer • Controller: Implements control algorithms (impedance, torque-based control). o Control Algorithms: Manages how the robotic system interacts with the user, providing two modes of operation namely, assist as needed and resistive.
## 4. Sensing and Actuation Layer • Sensors: Collect data on user performance and system status. o 2D Camera: capturing the vide stream of the user performing exercises in front of it o IMUs (Inertial Measurement Units): Track upper body and arm movement, position, and orientation, particularly important for locations where there is no line of sight by the camera, such as upper and lower back o Force/Torque Sensors: Measure interaction forces between the user and the robotic arm, measuring the pressure exerted by the user during the weight bearing exercises. o Encoders: Measure joint angles and positions.
• Actuators: Drive the robotic arm's movements and provide haptic feedback to the user. o Main Motor: Provides the necessary force to complete ROM movements. o System motors: Linear actuators and stepper motors may be used to automate the setup of the system in between ROM exercises o Haptic feedback actuators: small actuator motors that are placed on the user upper body to provide haptic feedback.
## 5. Data Processing Layer • Data Acquisition: Collects data from sensors.
• Data Processing: Filters, processes, and analyzes sensor data in real-time.
• Performance Metrics: Calculates key performance indicators such as repetition count, range of motion, and engagement levels.
## 6. Communication Layer • Wireless Communication: Enables data exchange between the robotic system and external devices or servers. o Protocols: Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, or other wireless communication protocols. o Communication needs to take place between sensors, application layer, controller, and the user interface.

evices or servers. o Protocols: Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, or other wireless communication protocols. o Communication needs to take place between sensors, application layer, controller, and the user interface.

## 7. Database Layer • Data Storage: Stores user performance data, system logs, and game progress. o Database Management System (DBMS): Manages the database operations, ensuring data integrity and accessibility. ## 1. Modular Design • Description: The robotic platform must have a modular design to allow easy customization and adaptability to different patient needs and recovery stages.

Safety • Description: The system must incorporate safety measures such as constraining the range of motion within human limits, setting comfortable thresholds for actuators' torque and speed, and including a deadman safety button. • Purpose: To ensure the patient's safety during use and interaction with the system, preventing injuries and enhancing the overall reliability of the system. • This requirement is designed for at 3 levels of mechanical, electrical, and software to ensure a secure environment o Mechanical Design: ▪ The design eliminates sharp edges to reduce the risk of injury. ▪ Physical stops are incorporated to prevent any joint from exceeding the anatomical limits of human motion, thus avoiding hyperextension or hyperflexion. o Electrical Design: ▪ The device includes two shut-off switches for enhanced safety. Mechanical & Electrical Design Progress Design Requirements Based on a comprehensive literature review of available rehabilitation robotics and conversation with clinicians, the following mechanical specifications have been established: ## 1. Initial setup time of less than five minutes, with set up time of less than 3 minutes in between exercises ## 2. Ability to operate and provide assistive torque within the prescribed ROM exercises. ## 3. Design features that facilitate a seamless transition between different ROM exercises. ## 4. A mechanism to allow for precise alignment between the robotic arm and the user's limb for each ROM exercise. ## 5. User-friendly setup that can be performed independently with minimal assistance. ## 6. Low-backlash gearing to enhance the smoothness of ROM exercises. ## 7.

Backdrivable transmissions to allow for more compliant motion. 8. Low inertia links to improve responsiveness, reduce energy consumption, and enhance control, enabling more precise movements. 9. High stiffness transmissions to minimize deflection, enhance positioning and stability, increase load capacity, and improve response times. Current Design idea/components The mechanical aspect of the platform can be broken down into 4 major parts: namely the base unit, the end effector, the wearable brace, and the handheld device. Below description of each is provided. Base Unit The base unit serves as the primary structural and control hub of the rehabilitation system, securely fastening to a table edge via a manual locking mechanism. It is designed to accommodate both wheelchair and regular chair seating by adjusting for different patient heights. To address significant height variability, the table itself must also be height-adjustable. The base unit provides a single degree of freedom for limb manipulation, with the flexibility to pivot or reposition the

ignificant height variability, the table itself must also be height-adjustable. The base unit provides a single degree of freedom for limb manipulation, with the flexibility to pivot or reposition the

device to facilitate multiple range-of-motion (ROM) exercises. Depending on final design decisions, these orientation changes may be performed either manually or through supplementary linear actuators or stepper motors, separate from the primary actuator that drives patient limb movement. Such actuators-if automated-would allow fine alignment of the motor axis with the patient's anatomical joint. For safety, the base unit includes both an emergency stop (E-stop) and provision for potential mechanical stops to protect the patient if control signals fail. Activating the E-stop immediately cuts power to motors, actuators, and any engaged electromagnets, while maintaining power to the embedded controller (e.g., Raspberry Pi). This design ensures that the user's limb can disengage from the device even under fault conditions. Clinical input indicates that patients typically progress from proximal to distal ROM exercises, motivating an arrangement sequence: shoulder abduction/adduction, shoulder flexion/extension, shoulder rotation, elbow flexion/extension, and finally wrist supination/pronation. Incorporating this clinical order into the design helps limit the number of mechanical reconfigurations between exercises. To accommodate widely varying limb sizes or joint alignments, the device's limb support is removable or adjustable and can be adapted to different patient anthropometrics using either tool-less or tool-based adjustments. The base unit also houses power distribution and other electrical components, including ventilation ports to mitigate overheating of internal electronics. Exposed ports provide access to Raspberry Pi connections such as USB and Ethernet, and integrated handles may be added to facilitate transport. Front-facing receptacles allow clamps to secure the base unit firmly against the table, ensuring stability throughout exercise sessions. In summary, this consolidated base unit design prioritizes patient safety, adaptability to a range of anatomical and exercise needs, and robust housing of all core power and control elements.

Figure 4 Inside of the base unit The end effector This intermediate section of the system lies between the base unit and the wearable brace, housing the primary mechanism for transitioning among different range-of-motion (ROM) exercises. Two design approaches are currently under evaluation. The first employs stepper motors or linear actuators for automated reconfiguration, while the second proposes a manual system requiring minimal user effort-ideally manageable with a single unimpaired hand. Additionally, this section provides the attachment point for the user's brace, which offers limb support and integrates wearable sensors and haptic modules. Velcro straps affix similar sensors around the trunk and sides, but the brace must allow users to disengage and free themselves independently, a key safety feature. Several potential attachment mechanisms were considered. Thumb screws were found cumbersome for users with limited strength or dexterity, requiring large handles for easy loosening and tightening. Velcro provided a simple concept but lacked durability, demanded two-handed operation, and risked imprecise sensor placement as noted during laboratory testing. A push-button latch delivered quick release but required careful alignment to secure. Finally, a solenoid-based design-using an electromagnet to hold the brace-emerged as a promising solution, enabling effortless attachment guided by the magnetic field and quick detachment with the press of a button. This method theoretically

electromagnet to hold the brace-emerged as a promising solution, enabling effortless attachment guided by the magnetic field and quick detachment with the press of a button. This method theoretically

minimizes the force and precision required from the user, and early reviews suggested it could provide the easiest "don on and don off" experience. Despite these advantages, the presence of a strong electromagnetic field raises concerns about interference with magnetometer-based inertial measurement units (IMUs). To address this, the team procured candidate electromagnets and commenced trials to gauge potential effects on sensor accuracy. IMU Testing with Electromagnets Bench testing was conducted to assess the interference of an Adafruit 25 kg holding-force electromagnet on a BNO055 inertial measurement unit (IMU), with particular focus on the IMU's magnetometer readings. The electromagnet was driven at 0.6 A and placed at various distances (15 cm, 9 cm, and 5 cm) from the IMU. An initial "idle" test established a baseline with no electromagnetic interference: the magnetometer output remained steady, aside from minor noise spikes. When the electromagnet was switched on at 15 cm separation (from 50 s to 120 s), the IMU displayed a small but discernible dip in its Z-axis reading. Reducing the gap to 9 cm produced a more pronounced drop, reflecting closer proximity and heightened magnetic field effects. At only 5 cm, the magnetometer output ceased entirely once the electromagnet energized, indicating severe interference well beyond the IMU's tolerance.

Preliminary calculations of magnetic field strength used an approximate cross-sectional area of: A=πr2with r=0.02m yielding A≈1.2566×10^-3 m^2. Under these parameters, the magnetic flux density BBB was estimated at around 0.707 T, sufficiently high to disrupt magnetometer functionality at close range. To mitigate such interference, multiple shielding materials are under consideration, including MuMetal, high-permeability alloys, ferrites, and steel/iron sheets. While 1-2 mm thickness can significantly attenuate low-frequency magnetic fields, it requires careful design integration due to cost, weight, and spatial constraints. An alternative strategy is maintaining a minimum distance, on the order of 15 cm, between the electromagnet and the IMU, which may not be possible. However, if the design dictates closer proximity, a multi-layer shielding approach may be necessary to preserve magnetometer accuracy. Future work will center on evaluating the feasibility and practicality of installing dedicated magnetic shields around the electromagnet or testing its effect on the IMU data. It may be necessary to consider alternative options for the attachment mechanism.

Figure 10 IMU magnetometer reading when electromagnet is 9 cm away Wearable Limb Brace and Sensor straps A critical element of the rehabilitation system is the brace mechanism, which couples the impaired arm to the robot while supporting safe and effective exercise transitions. Foremost, the brace must prevent the arm from slipping or falling during movement, and should remain sufficiently lightweight to avoid straining the patient's limb. When the patient disengages from the robot-whether for switching exercises or responding to a quick-release event-they should ideally support the impaired limb with their healthy arm, ensuring controlled withdrawal without loss of stability. From a mechanical standpoint, the brace comprises orthotic shells sized for the upper and lower arm, combined with alignment features to match the patient's joint with the robot's actuator axis. This alignment can be refined through stepper motor adjustments on the base unit. Various attachment concepts have been evaluated, including double-sided Velcro straps and electromagnets, to secure the brace to the device. In addition, a sliding connector can be introduced between the upper and lower arm shells, enabling or restricting elbow motion as required by specific exercises.

For tasks needing a locked elbow, the connector is slid into place; for those requiring flexion, it is removed with a simple sliding action. This approach supports arm elevation, abduction/adduction, rotation, elbow flexion/extension, and wrist supination/pronation. Sensor integration is also a priority. Up to six haptic modules and six IMUs are to be placed across the body, with at least two devices (forearm and upper arm) potentially attached directly to the brace. The rest can be distributed as needed using additional wearable components. Maintaining contact between the haptic modules and the user's skin (e.g., through flexible clothing braces) is essential for consistent feedback during motion. To ensure proper sizing, the brace's length and fit draw on anthropometric data indicating minimum arm and forearm lengths of approximately 0.27 m and 0.21 m, respectively [6]. Each brace is sized to 70 percent of these measurements, preventing it from encroaching on the patient's actual joint while preserving freedom of movement. For example, the smallest aluminum link for the forearm would measure around 147 mm (70 percent of 210 mm). Overall, the brace's design

the patient's actual joint while preserving freedom of movement. For example, the smallest aluminum link for the forearm would measure around 147 mm (70 percent of 210 mm). Overall, the brace's design

focuses on secure attachment, reliable quick-release in emergencies, alignment flexibility, and comfortable yet robust sensor integration, all while keeping the user's arm adequately supported throughout each exercise phase. Figure 11The current brace design Handheld Device The handheld device functions as a standalone piece from the robotic system and it is used for performing static and dynamic weight bearing exercises, allowing the patient to put pressure/weight on it and slide it on the surface of the table. Several grip shapes are under consideration, including a simple cylindrical grip to accommodate a curved hand position. This attachment can be tailored to individual users either by custom-manufacturing (for example, 3Dprinting a grip sized for each patient and screwing it onto the device). The device integrates a force sensor to measure downward force exerted by the patient's hand, supporting real-time feedback and data capture. This sensor links to a driver board and is controlled by an ESP32C3 microcontroller, which handles communication over Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) to the central Raspberry Pi. A 1,000 mAh LiPo battery will power the system, with an on/off switch and a status LED for user convenience. Future improvements include refining the grip design to incorporate multiple smaller pressure sensors distributed across the palm and fingers, providing higher-resolution grip data in place of a single, centrally located force sensor. This approach promises more granular insight into how each finger contributes to the grip, potentially improving the therapeutic value. The flexible top portion of the grip can further enhance comfort and sensor access. Overall, the hand attachment design aims to combine ergonomic support, robust force measurement, and flexible adaptivity for different patient needs.

Figure 12 CAD drawing of the handheld device Figure 13 Farce sensor positioned at the bottom of the handheld device Control System Design A pivotal requirement for effective robot-mediated therapy lies in an adaptable, cooperative control solution that aligns with each patient's progress. Our rehabilitation system's control architecture will therefore focus on establishing a compliant human-robot interaction. Its central objective is to adapt assistance and resistance levels in real time, according to the patient's evolving performance and volitional effort. This adaptability not only minimizes undesirable interaction forces but also promotes patient engagement and ensures task completion. A key element underpinning these interactions is compliant motion, which enables the robotic system to both initiate movement and accommodate external forces, thereby maintaining smooth, natural exercise dynamics. The system features two main operational modes, each addressing specific stages or requirements in the rehabilitation process. Two primary "training modalities" define distinct highlevel behaviors:

s. The system features two main operational modes, each addressing specific stages or requirements in the rehabilitation process. Two primary "training modalities" define distinct highlevel behaviors:

1) Assistance. In this mode, the robot compensates for the limb's weight and dynamic loads, providing partial support during movements. The level of help is continuously adjusted based on the user's effort, ensuring engagement while preventing overexertion. 2) Resistance. This strategy intentionally elevates the difficulty of movements, thereby promoting more intensive muscle activation and enhancing motor control learning. By mimicking conventional resistance exercises (e.g., using weights or bands), this modality helps build strength and coordination. Control architecture: Many existing control systems for rehabilitation robots rely on impedance-based designs, wherein virtual springs and dampers are introduced to achieve compliant physical human-robot interaction. Building on this framework, the proposed architecture determines the required torque for assistance or resistance at a high level, while a lowerlevel controller enforces the commanded torque. Following the approach in [7], a singlejoint elbow device integrating high-ratio gear motors and a dedicated torque sensor enables explicit impedance control, thus offering precise torque regulation and low mechanical impedance. The software executes in real time within a nested-loop architecture: an inner, high-frequency torque loop leverages load-cell feedback to reject gearbox friction and applies feedforward compensation for static and viscous losses, reducing perceived impedance and enhancing backdrivability. Meanwhile, the outer impedance loop governs virtual stiffness, damping, and gravity compensation, adjusting for any deviations from the desired motion. This configuration provides a dynamically transparent, adaptive interface that can seamlessly span a range of training from assistive to resistive exercises while maintaining stability and encouraging voluntary user engagement. Figure 14 Control architecture being considered, taken from [7]

ly span a range of training from assistive to resistive exercises while maintaining stability and encouraging voluntary user engagement. Figure 14 Control architecture being considered, taken from [7]

In a similar way, the impedance model must be expanded to encompass additional ranges of motion, with corresponding control loops designed for each exercise. Actuator Selection Based on the literature review [6], the torque required for the targeted range-ofmotion (ROM) exercises spans roughly 20-70 N, with a typical demand around 45 N. To meet these requirements while maintaining a smooth, controlled movement, an actuator must provide sufficient torque output and exhibit minimal backlash. Backdrivability is another desirable feature, ideally achieved by a low gear ratio, although this objective can conflict with the need for higher torque. A cost-effective solution with a compact mechanical footprint is also essential. After evaluating commercial options, the Cubemars AK80-64 motor (https://www.cubemars.com/goods-1143-AK80-64.html) was selected, as it strikes a balance among these criteria. It delivers a nominal torque of 48 Nm (with a 120 Nm maximum), maintains backlash below 1°, and employs a planetary gear with a 64:1 ratio for partial backdrivability. While this gear ratio may still be high for an impaired arm, the controller's servo and MIT modes, combined with software-based control strategies, can mitigate some limitations. Priced under $1,000, the AK80-64 thus provides a practical compromise of cost, performance, and form factor.

Torque Sensor Selection Various torque sensors (load cells) are under consideration to ensure accurate torque measurement up to a maximum motor torque of 120 Nm. Ideally, the chosen sensor should also be low-profile and compact. Given that multi-axis (e.g., 6-DOF) solutions are prohibitively expensive, a single-axis sensor mounted at the motor output was deemed more cost-effective. Among the off-the-shelf options explored, a flange-type force-torque sensor rated at 100 Nm emerged as the preferred choice (https://www.ato.com/force-torque-sensor). Although this rating does not fully match the motor's 120 Nm upper limit, the expected operating range (based on the literature) remains well below 100 Nm. Consequently, selecting a 100 Nm device should enable more precise measurement within the target torque region. Mechanical Motor Disengagement Mechanism For patient safety, it is critical to have a mechanical safeguard that automatically disengages the motor once it exceeds a predetermined rotational speed, preventing accidental arm rotation. A preliminary investigation into suitable mechanisms revealed that centrifugal clutches are commonly used for automatic disengagement at specific RPM values. In such systems, outward-moving weights reach a threshold where they separate the clutch from the output shaft. However, our application requires a clutch that can sustain up to 120 Nm of torque at speeds around 30 rpm, which is far below conventional operating ranges. Achieving reliable actuation at lower speeds demands careful selection of weight masses and springs.

Upon inquiring with Helander Motors, we found that no standard products support such low-speed, high-torque requirements; their offerings only handle 60 Nm at speeds in the low hundreds of rpm. Instead, they recommended exploring spring, sprag, or tooth-ratcheting clutches, potentially relying on ultra-lightweight springs to accommodate slower RPMs. Consequently,

in the low hundreds of rpm. Instead, they recommended exploring spring, sprag, or tooth-ratcheting clutches, potentially relying on ultra-lightweight springs to accommodate slower RPMs. Consequently,

further development will need to focus on identifying or devising a custom mechanism optimized for these atypical torque and speed parameters. Haptic Module The haptic module is designed to provide tactile feedback to the user through a series of small, wearable devices. The system currently comprises six modules, each equipped with a microcontroller (ESP32C3). These modules are also outfitted with a buzzer, a haptic motor, and a rechargeable battery, enhancing the wireless capability and user experience. Communication is facilitated via Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) connections with a central Raspberry Pi, ensuring efficient synchronization across the modules. Each module houses a 1000mAh battery that provides approximately four hours of charge at maximum power utilization. The objective of this project is to optimize the placement of haptic modules across key body locations to provide real-time haptic and auditory feedback, guiding users to adjust their posture and movement patterns during physical exercises. These modules serve dual functions: initiating movement cues and delivering corrective feedback to mitigate compensatory behaviors. Specifically, modules positioned on each side of the upper hip are designed to alert users of lateral tilting, a common issue in stroke survivors, suggesting a collapse toward the affected side.

Additionally, a module near the tailbone alerts the user for issues regarding the spinal alignment and trunk posture, addressing tendencies to hunch or slouch. Further modules are placed on the shoulder blade, forearm, and upper arm. Here, slow vibrations prompt the initiation of movements, while faster vibrations coupled with auditory signals (beeping) provide immediate feedback for correcting or halting compensatory actions. Although the current plan details these specific placements and modes of operation, adjustments may be necessary based on outcomes from ongoing user testing studies. The enclosures for these modules have initially been fabricated using PLA material with thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) at the contact surface with the user. TPU, known for its flexibility and rubber-like properties, significantly amplifies the transmission of vibrations, thereby improving the feedback efficacy and the overall functionality of the haptic modules. Although PLA is used for initial enclosures, future prototypes will consider ABS or PETG to minimize brittleness and absorb vibration more robustly. Custom PCB board was designed for the haptic modules. The ESP32C3 microcontroller was selected for its small footprint, built-in BLE, on-board LiPo battery charging, and straightforward programmability-features that help streamline integration and reduce system size. A transistor circuit is required to power the haptic motor, as it can draw up to 80 mA, exceeding the microcontroller's GPIO limit of 40 mA.

A 2N2222 transistor and parallel diode protect the circuit from voltage spikes. The resistor value is chosen to supply sufficient base current, ensuring adequate motor drive while safeguarding the microcontroller. The LiPo battery (3.3 V, 1000 mAh) offers around four hours of runtime at peak load and is combined with a softenable circuit so it can be charged even when the device is switched off. Initial Bluetooth connectivity tests confirm reliable close-range pairing between each module and a smartphone app, demonstrating the viability of the BLE-based networking approach. The future work includes refining enclosures that provide more space for components and stronger resistance to mechanical stress are planned. User trials will guide decisions on module placement,

vibration intensity, and beep patterns, with the overarching goal of refining both user comfort and therapeutic outcomes. On the software side, the haptic modules will be triggered by a real-time posture-tracking engine that combines data from inertial measurement units (IMUs) and potentially video input to detect misalignments. A Raspberry Pi serves as the central communication node, hosting a Python-based server-client architecture that receives and processes the posture-tracking information. When an alignment issue is detected, the Pi sends activation commands to the haptic modules via BLE, where low-level control is handled in C code. This design ensures rapid corrective feedback to users, enabling seamless integration of pose detection and haptic guidance. Figure 15 Haptic module circuit schematic

Figure 16 Haptic module built IMU Sensors This project leverages inertial measurement units (IMUs) to capture user orientation and movement in real time, with particular emphasis on monitoring the affected arm, shoulder and the upper trunk positions. An IMU typically combines a 3D accelerometer to measure linear acceleration, a 3D gyroscope to measure angular velocity, and a 3D magnetometer to track ambient magnetic fields

e upper trunk positions. An IMU typically combines a 3D accelerometer to measure linear acceleration, a 3D gyroscope to measure angular velocity, and a 3D magnetometer to track ambient magnetic fields

for absolute heading reference. In principle, integrating the gyroscope output over time yields orientation changes, and double integration of the accelerometer output provides position estimates; however, raw integration is prone to substantial drift when subjected to real-world noise. To mitigate this, sensor fusion algorithms, such as Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), continuously reconcile gyroscopic estimates with accelerometer and magnetometer references. This process includes a "prediction step," which uses angular velocity to update the current orientation, and an "update step," which corrects the predicted state using gravity and magnetic north measurements. Although Euler angles (roll, pitch, yaw) can be derived from these fused results, quaternions are frequently preferred because they avoid the gimbal lock singularities inherent to Euler-based representations. Kalman filters and similar fusion approaches (e.g., Rauch-TungStriebel smoothing) generally require a brief warm-up period with minimal movement and magnetic disturbance to stabilize initial orientation estimates. In practical usage, additional filtering of the sensor outputs such as low-pass or high-pass filters to help remove noise and spurious spikes. Initially, the BNO055 9-axis IMU was used as a budget-friendly option supporting wired I2C or SPI protocols (https://www.adafruit.com/product/2472).

Achievements included parallel I2C connections for two IMUs, onboard calibration routines, and firmware to convert quaternion data to Euler angles. However, static tests showed drift of around 7° in the roll measurement over ten minutes, along with sporadic spikes and magnetometer calibration challenges. The presence of electromagnets near the system further complicated usage of the BNO055's magnetometer. These findings prompted a shift toward the XIO 5G IMU-3 (x-io Technologies, Bristol, UK), which demonstrates improved accuracy (±1°), more reliable calibration procedures and builtin complement filter using Madgwick algorithm to minimize drift, and the option to disable the magnetometer if electromagnetic interference is present. Such flexibility is critical in rehabilitation scenarios where local magnetic fields may be distorted. Sensor outputs included quaternions, rotation matrix, Euler angles (0~2000o/s), linear acceleration (0~16 g), and compass heading information (0~1300 μT). We are using 6 IMU sensors simultaneously to track the upper body movements. The sampling rate of the IMUs were set based on the literature to be 50 Hz as it seems sufficient for human activity recognition tasks [8]. Data collection was performed using six IMU-3 sensors placed at key anatomical sites: the wrist, lower arm, upper arm, shoulder, upper back, and lower back. The lower back sensor serves as the reference origin, enabling construction of the subject's kinematic chain and facilitating calculation of relative limb segment motion.

In parallel, a marker-based video motion capture system (considered the gold standard) recorded the same movements. By comparing angle measurements derived from the IMU data to those obtained via the video system, we aims to assess the accuracy and reliability of the IMU-based tracking approach. Early results indicate close agreement in many ranges of motion, though further data analysis is underway to quantify any discrepancies and refine calibration procedures. Future development will investigate merging IMU-derived information with video-based tracking to enhance overall system robustness and precision. Additionally, rule-based algorithms are planned to detect compensatory movements in real time, allowing immediate feedback to be delivered to the patient. The ultimate goal involves integrating the sensor suite with a game engine to track performance during exercises.

These are tests for IMU current sensing for BNO055 Power Distribution The electrical power distribution unit was designed by focusing on how power is distributed among the robot's actuators, electromagnets, sensors, and the Raspberry Pi, while also taking some safety measures. A 48 V DC, 25 A (1200 W) power supply was selected to ensure sufficient headroom above the estimated peak demand of approximately 1,034 W, minimizing the risk of overheating. From this supply, DC/DC buck converters generate 12 V (up to 10 A) and 5 V (up to 10 A) rails. The 12 V line powers the NEMA 17 stepper motors, linear actuators, and auxiliary components, while the 5 V line supports the Raspberry Pi, electromagnets, and potentially certain sensor modules. Each major actuator or electromagnet employs a dedicated relay or transistor-based circuit that can be deactivated via an emergency stop (E-stop) or selective release switch. This arrangement ensures the user can disconnect motors and electromagnets quickly if needed, leaving the Raspberry Pi powered to maintain system logging and user interface functions. Fuses are sized per device category-ranging from a 20 A fuse on the 48 V AK80-64 motor line, down to a 1 A fuse on the 5 V electromagnets-to safeguard against short-circuits or overload conditions. The robot also includes an upstream 15 A fast-blow fuse on the AC input side. Ultimately, this design strategy integrates high-current-actuator reliability with controlled voltage step-down, redundant safety switches, and overcurrent protection to form a stable, operator-safe electrical platform.

Modular and Iterative Software Design The system is built with a modular architecture to allow for easy updates and modifications, minimizing code redundancy and promoting scalability. This approach ensures that future features can be seamlessly integrated without affecting existing functionality. Intuitive User Interface (UI) The UI is designed to be user-friendly, requiring minimal training for patients and therapists. The interface aims to minimize confusion, presenting a low learning curve to ensure users can easily navigate through rehabilitation exercises and reports. Gamified Rehabilitation The system will include rehabilitation exercises that are gamified to align with clinical goals, providing patients with engaging and therapeutic tasks that enhance their recovery experience. Meaningful and Personalized Feedback The software will offer personalized feedback to users based on their individual performance data. This feature ensures that feedback is relevant and actionable, promoting better engagement with rehabilitation tasks. Engagement and Motivation Features To keep users motivated and engaged throughout their rehabilitation process, the system will integrate reward mechanisms, dynamic difficulty adjustment, achievement tracking, and social features. These elements aim to maintain long-term adherence to the rehabilitation program. In addition, providing an opportunity to socialize and having a multiplayer ability is a crucial factor to improve technology adoption. Key Design Elements Based on relevant literature and patient needs, we have identified the following critical design elements for rehabilitation games: 1. Real-Time Posture Monitoring and Feedback The software must ensure that users maintain a healthy and safe posture during exercises. Real-time feedback is provided to correct poor posture (e.g., hunching, leaning), preventing potential injury or improper movement patterns. Implementation:

healthy and safe posture during exercises. Real-time feedback is provided to correct poor posture (e.g., hunching, leaning), preventing potential injury or improper movement patterns. Implementation:

• Haptic Feedback: Vibrations at specific body points (e.g., shoulders, back, or hips) cue users to correct posture. (Further details in the electrical subsection). • Audio Feedback: Beeping sounds and descriptive audio instructions alert users to posture issues and guide them through corrections. (Further details in the electrical subsection). • Visual Feedback: A 2D body avatar with color-coded feedback (e.g., red for poor posture, green for correct posture) visually represents misaligned body parts, accompanied by textual warnings in the game interface. • Audiovisual Instructional feedback: provide correctional short instructional prompts while playing the game to promote performance improvement Metrics for Success: • Total Number of Correctional Feedback Prompts: A reduction in the number of prompts over time indicates improved posture habits. • Success Rate of Posture Correction: An 80% or higher success rate in correcting posture after feedback demonstrates effective guidance. • Correction Time: A decrease in the average time it takes for a user to adjust their posture suggests faster responsiveness and improved awareness. 2. Customizability and Personalization The software must allow users to create customizable exercise routines tailored to their specific rehabilitation goals.

Patients can set and adjust training objectives, including range of motion (ROM), accuracy, and movement speed, while the system tracks and reports on individual performance metrics in real-time. Implementation: • Goal Setting: Patients can define personalized rehabilitation targets, such as ROM, accuracy, and speed, and modify exercise settings based on their preferences. • Progress Tracking: Performance history is tracked and displayed through meaningful visual representations, such as progress graphs. Metrics for Success: • Customization Frequency: 80% or more of users should modify the parameters of interest, demonstrating the effective use of customization features. • Feedback Personalization Rating: Users will rate the quality of feedback personalization on a scale of 1 to 7, with an average rating of 5 or above indicating successful implementation. 3. Engagement and Motivation Features To enhance long-term engagement, the software will integrate features designed to motivate users through rewards, dynamic difficulty adjustment, and social interaction. These elements help maintain user interest and improve adherence to the rehabilitation program.

tures designed to motivate users through rewards, dynamic difficulty adjustment, and social interaction. These elements help maintain user interest and improve adherence to the rehabilitation program.

Implementation: • Reward-Punishment System: In-game rewards (e.g., points or bonuses) will be awarded for reaching rehabilitation targets, while penalties (e.g., point deductions) will encourage improved performance.
• Achievement Badges: Users earn badges for significant milestones, fostering a sense of accomplishment.
• Leaderboard: A leaderboard displaying personal top scores will motivate users to achieve their best performance.
• Dynamic Difficulty Adjustment (DDA): The difficulty will automatically adjust based on user performance, increasing when the user demonstrates proficiency and decreasing when they struggle. (TO DO IN STUDY 2 PHASE) • Cooperative and Competitive Modes: Social engagement through cooperative and competitive game modes will foster collaboration or healthy competition among users. (TO DO IN STUDY 3 PHASE) Metrics for Success: • Login Frequency: An average of 3 or more logins per week indicates consistent user engagement.
• Level Progression: At least 70% of users should demonstrate steady progression through game levels or difficulty milestones.
• Length of Login Sessions: An average session length of 15 minutes or more suggests sustained user engagement.
• Player Social Engagement: At least 50% of users should participate in cooperative or competitive modes weekly, indicating that social features are effectively enhancing engagement. Software architecture: The software framework of the rehabilitation system is designed as a multilayer modular architecture to support scalability and flexibility. The framework is predominantly developed in Python, integrating a variety of specialized modules tailored to the rehabilitation process. Unity is employed for the development of interactive rehabilitation games, enhancing patient engagement through gamified experiences. The system also incorporates specific firmware for controller functionalities, ensuring smooth operation and integration of hardware components. The core python framework includes the following modules: • GUI • Controller • Vision Module • Posture Monitoring • Games

Graphical User Interface (GUI) The GUI is developed using Streamlit, a powerful Python library that enables rapid deployment of user interfaces. This interface facilitates user login, displays game selection options, and allows users to input and adjust their profile settings, including session time and repetition goals. After initial setup, users are directed through a calibration process to customize the game environment to their specific needs. User Authentication and Profile Setup On application startup, users log in (or create an account) via Streamlit. A locally stored authentication token-valid for 60 minutes-manages session access. Upon account creation, a dedicated user folder is generated in the users directory. This folder stores calibration data, performance logs, and credentials. Exercise Selection and Calibration After successful login, users choose a target exercise (e.g., Shoulder Abduction, Elbow Flexion) and are prompted to perform calibration if needed. Calibration datacaptured via the vision module-helps map actual limb motion to in-game angles. The system saves these ranges (max/min angles) to a temporary folder (game config) and, once complete, relocates them to the user's personal directory. Streamlit Structure Individual pages reside in the pages folder, each fulfilling distinct roles (e.g., login, calibration, exercise selection, game launch, and results). Streamlit's sequential rendering means the order of function calls directly determines UI element arrangement.

distinct roles (e.g., login, calibration, exercise selection, game launch, and results). Streamlit's sequential rendering means the order of function calls directly determines UI element arrangement. Vision and Posture Monitoring Module The vision module utilizes Mediapipe, a vision-based framework, to track upper body movements, crucial for monitoring the user's posture during exercises. This module ensures accurate detection and real-time feedback on the user's posture, alerting the system to any deviations from desired postures through visual, auditory, or haptic feedback. Modes The vision module runs as a Python script (vision.py) that can operate in one of four modes: • Mirror: Displays live video without angle computations. • Calibration: Computes angles for each posture but ignores user-specific max/min ranges. • Calibration Adjusted Input: Scales angles by the user's calibrated ROM. • Calibration Adjusted Input with Posture Monitoring: Adds posture-check logic to detect unsafe or suboptimal positions (e.g., shoulder abduction errors), sending "stop," "slow," or "good" signals to the Unity game. Posture Angle Definitions The system calculates angles (e.g., shoulder rotation, abduction, flexion, elbow flexion, wrist pronation) by referencing the user's anatomical landmarks. Angles are typically derived relative to Earth gravity (-y axis) or the user's body axes, then passed to Unity via UDP on port 5000 (for game input) and 5006 (for posture monitoring).

Calibration and Data Flow A critical component of the system, the calibration and configuration layer, is responsible for customizing the virtual environment based on individual patient needs. It stores virtual environment preferences and kinematic data linked to patient identifiers, allowing for personalized settings that require calibration only once per patient unless their physical condition changes. Users often stand (or sit) in front of a camera, performing movements to define max and min angles for each target posture. The module outputs JSON data containing posture angles; Unity calibrations scenes interpret these data, providing visual feedback and storing final calibration outcomes. Game Development and Interaction Games are developed in Unity and are designed to integrate seamlessly with the Python backbone. The game controller receives updated parameters from the calibration layer and adjusts the game scenarios accordingly. This interaction ensures that the games

rate seamlessly with the Python backbone. The game controller receives updated parameters from the calibration layer and adjusts the game scenarios accordingly. This interaction ensures that the games

Game Control Each Unity game includes a corresponding "controller" class, responsible for adapting gameplay difficulty based on real-time performance: ## 1. Static: Retains fixed game parameters.
## 2. Logic: Simple if-then adjustments (e.g., if user reaches a target three times consecutively, increase difficulty).
## 3. Advanced Logic: Unused in this build but reserved for more complex heuristics.
4. Reinforcement Learning: Placeholder for potential future ML-based difficulty scaling. These controllers draw configuration data (initial difficulty, exercise mode, etc.) from JSON files in controllers/controller config.json and feed updated parameters back to the Unity application. Performance Tracking and Feedback The performance tracking module plays a pivotal role by identifying compensatory movements and interacting with the Unity engine to adjust game mechanics and feedback accordingly. This module is integral to providing real-time audiovisual feedback and enhancing the therapeutic experience by adapting to the user's performance throughout the session. Firmware and Sensor Integration The system includes firmware specifically designed for controlling actuators and reading data from various sensors managed by a Raspberry Pi. This setup ensures that all sensor data is accurately captured and utilized for adjusting game dynamics and providing feedback. The details of the firmware related to each sensor is discussed within that sensor's subsection. The Flappy Bird The Flappy Bird Game is an interactive rehabilitation exercise that integrates upper limb movement into gameplay. Players control a bird's vertical movement by performing arm exercises such as shoulder flexion/extension or abduction/adduction. The bird ascends when the player raises their arm and gradually descends when at rest. The objective is to navigate through gaps between obstacles while maintaining smooth and controlled movements. Players earn points by successfully passing through openings, while collisions result in game failure. Adjustable difficulty settings, including obstacle spacing and movement sensitivity, allow for individualized exercise progression and engagement. The following games are at ideation phase and have not been developed: The One Mole Whac-A-Mole The One Mole Whac-A-Mole Game is an interactive rehabilitation exercise designed to engage users in weight-bearing arm movements through gamification. Players control a hammer by performing specific upper limb exercises, such as shoulder, elbow, and wrist weight-bearing movements, to target and strike appearing moles. The game requires users to apply sufficient force, adjustable via sensor settings, for the hammer to register a hit. Moles appear in fixed or randomized positions for a limited time, and successfully hitting a mole awards points while missing results in point deductions. Electrical schematics: Detailed electrical circuit design can be found: CIRCUITS Haptic Module Below is the Haptic Module PCB design: Below is the BOM for one Haptic Module: Anatomy The upper limb is composed of three parts: the arm, the forearm, and the hand. The joint complex where the ends of the humerus, scapula, and clavicle bones meet is called the shoulder, which allows movement in the three anatomical planes (sagittal, frontal, and transverse). The shoulder movements can be categorized as flexion-extension, adduction-abduction, internalexternal rotation, horizontal flexion-extension, and complementary protraction-retraction movements. The upper arm is composed of the humerus, on which different triceps and biceps muscles are inserted in order to perform the move ments produced from the shoulder. The arm is joined to the forearm through the elbow joint. The elbow is the intermediate joint of the upper extremity. It is composed of three joints, which allow flexion and extension movements (humerus-ulna), distribute the load-bearing forces (humerus-radius), and transmit pronation and supin ation movements (ulna-radius) to the wrist.

Real-Time IMU-Based Tracking of Upper-Limb Kinematics and Compensatory Movements During Shoulder Rehabilitation manuscript for marker-based validation Abstract Home-based upper-limb rehabilitation requires measurement systems that can quantify both prescribed joint-level range of motion and compensatory movement strategies with sufficient accuracy to support real-time feedback. Optical motion capture remains the reference standard for kinematic validation, but its cost and environmental constraints limit deployment outside the laboratory. Inertial measurement units (IMUs) and OpenSim-based inverse kinematics offer a promising alternative, yet translation to shoulder rehabilitation remains limited by sensor-to-segment calibration, scapula-inclusive modeling, and the need to detect clinically meaningful compensations rather than only endpoint success. This paper presents a prospective validation study for a real-time upper-body tracking system that integrates six body-mounted xIMU3 sensors and a customized OpenSenseRT/OpenSim workflow. The planned validation compares IMU-derived estimates of shoulder abduction/adduction, shoulder flexion/extension, shoulder internal/external rotation, elbow flexion/extension, and forearm pronation/supination against manually synchronized marker-based motion capture, while also quantifying trunk flexion/extension, lateral bending, axial rotation, shoulder elevation/depression and protraction/retraction, and lateral elbow displacement as compensatory strategies commonly adopted by individuals with limited range of movement. The biomechanical model is built from complementary contributions by OpenSim, the Rajagopal full-body model, the Seth scapulothoracic formulation, and recent augmented upper-body modeling work. We hypothesize that the proposed system will demonstrate good agreement with marker-based kinematics for trunk motion, shoulder flexion/abduction, and elbow flexion, with larger error expected for shoulder axial rotation and forearm pronation/supination. If successful, this study will provide the validation foundation for the ongoing translation of the system to mixedreality, home-based post-stroke rehabilitation with real-time movement-quality

ation. If successful, this study will provide the validation foundation for the ongoing translation of the system to mixedreality, home-based post-stroke rehabilitation with real-time movement-quality

feedback. Keywords: IMU; OpenSim; OpenSenseRT; upper-limb rehabilitation; compensatory movements; shoulder kinematics; marker-based validation; post-stroke rehabilitation 1. Introduction Upper-limb impairment after stroke remains a major barrier to independence, and a large proportion of recovery depends on sustained, high-quality practice beyond formal therapy. Immersive and mixed-reality rehabilitation platforms can increase engagement and motivation, but their clinical value depends on whether they can quantify movement quality rather than only task completion [18,19]. That distinction is especially important in home-based rehabilitation, where patients may achieve a nominal task goal using compensatory trunk, shoulder-girdle, or elbow strategies that reduce the therapeutic value of practice [10-12]. Kinematic assessment is attractive because it can separate restitution from compensation and characterize how movement is produced, not just whether a task is completed. Systematic work in post-stroke rehabilitation has emphasized the importance of joint angles, inter-joint coordination, and trunk displacement, while also noting that methods, tasks, and reported metrics remain highly heterogeneous across studies [10].

Technology-focused reviews have similarly shown that compensation detection during stroke upper-extremity rehabilitation is clinically relevant but still methodologically fragmented, with many systems relying on coarse classification or task-specific heuristics rather than a biomechanically interpretable joint-level model [11,12]. Optical motion capture remains the laboratory reference standard for such measurements, yet it is expensive, space-bound, and poorly suited to routine home deployment. IMUs provide a compelling alternative because they are wearable, low-cost, and can stream orientation data in real time. Current evidence suggests that IMUs can achieve good agreement with optical motion capture for several upper-extremity degrees of freedom, especially sagittal-plane motions, while performance is less stable for complex multiplanar and axial rotations [6,13,14]. In a recent meta-analysis of upper-extremity range-of-motion validity, concurrent validity was excellent for shoulder flexion-extension and elbow flexion-extension, good to excellent for shoulder abduction-adduction and elbow pronation-supination, and less consistent for shoulder internal-external rotation [13]. Despite this promise, translation of IMU-based upper-body tracking to rehabilitation settings remains limited by three technical bottlenecks. First, the accuracy of upper-limb IMU pipelines is highly sensitive to sensor-to-segment calibration, including the rotational offset between each sensor and the anatomical frame it is meant to represent [7,8]. Second, many generic full-body models

is highly sensitive to sensor-to-segment calibration, including the rotational offset between each sensor and the anatomical frame it is meant to represent [7,8]. Second, many generic full-body models

simplify the upper body in ways that are acceptable for gait but insufficient for scapula-inclusive shoulder rehabilitation, where shoulder-girdle and trunk compensations are central outcomes [2,3,9]. Third, most published IMU systems focus on estimating a subset of joint angles, whereas home rehabilitation needs a compact, interpretable set of both target movement variables and compensatory movement variables. OpenSim provides a natural modeling framework for this problem because it supports musculoskeletal modeling, inverse kinematics, and transparent extension of models and analyses [1]. The OpenSenseRT workflow demonstrated that realtime IMU-driven inverse kinematics can achieve upper-extremity errors on the order of approximately 5.6 degrees on a Fugl-Meyer task while remaining low-cost and open-source [5]. However, shoulder rehabilitation raises additional modeling demands. The Rajagopal full-body model is a widely used foundation but lumps parts of the upper body in ways that limit detailed shoulder-girdle tracking [2]. The Seth scapulothoracic model introduced an ellipsoid-based joint that more accurately captures scapular motion during shoulder movements [3], and recent augmented full-body work has shown that adding upper-body joints materially improves tracking and reduces dynamic inconsistency during complex movement [9].

The current paper is to validate the real-time IMU/OpenSim tracking pipeline against manually synchronized marker-based motion capture in scripted shoulder, elbow, wrist, trunk, and compensation tasks before the system is deployed more broadly in home rehabilitation platform for real time movement tracking. Accordingly, the paper focuses on the kinematic validity of the sensing-andmodeling stack rather than on intervention efficacy. ## 2. Main study contributions • A scapula-inclusive, upper-body OpenSim model configured for real-time IMU-driven inverse kinematics and designed around clinically meaningful rehabilitation variables. • A protocol-based validation framework that compares IMU-derived joint angles and compensation metrics against synchronized marker-based motion capture across repeated scripted ROM and compensation trials. • A translational bridge from laboratory validation to future mixed-reality, home-based post-stroke rehabilitation with real-time feedback on movement quality.
