## 3. Methods 3.1 Study design This study is designed as a prospective validation study comparing a realtime IMU-based upper-body kinematic tracking pipeline with a marker-based optical motion capture reference. The immediate validation cohort is with healthy adults. The validation targets both primary upper-limb joint angles and secondary compensatory movement metrics relevant to shoulder rehabilitation.
3.2 System overview The proposed system estimates upper-body kinematics in real time using six body-mounted IMUs placed on the pelvis, upper back, scapula, upper arm, forearm, and hand of the impaired or tested side. The pipeline is based on a modified OpenSenseRT/OpenSim architecture in which streaming quaternion orientations are supplied to a customized upper-body model and solved frame-byframe using inverse kinematics. The system is intended to operate on a Raspberry Pi 5 and to support integration with mixed-reality exergames and custom haptic feedback modules, although the present validation study focuses specifically on kinematic accuracy rather than game outcomes or feedback efficacy. Figure 1: IMU/OpenSim workflow for upper-body inverse kinematics.
3.3 Participants Participants in the initial validation phase are 10 neurologically intact adults recruited to perform repeated calibration, range-of-motion, and scripted compensation trials.
3.4 Wearable hardware and sensor placement Body-mounted data are collected using six xIMU3 sensors, each providing triaxial accelerometer, gyroscope, and magnetometer measurements internally fused to quaternions in real time using the Madgwick algorithm. Sensors are secured

using six xIMU3 sensors, each providing triaxial accelerometer, gyroscope, and magnetometer measurements internally fused to quaternions in real time using the Madgwick algorithm. Sensors are secured

using elastic straps or adhesive interfaces to reduce sensor motion relative to the segment. The pelvis sensor defines the root orientation of the kinematic chain, consistent with OpenSenseRT's base-sensor paradigm. Role in IK Primary motions Sensor Segment chain informed xIMU3-1 Pelvis Root/world Lumbar/trunk angles orientation and global chain anchor alignment xIMU3-2 Upper back / Thorax Thorax thorax orientation flexion-extension, constraint lateral bending, axial rotation xIMU3-3 Scapula Scapular Shoulder segment elevation/depression, constraint protraction/retraction xIMU3-4 Upper arm Humeral Shoulder segment flexion-extension, constraint abduction-adduction, internal-external rotation xIMU3-5 Forearm Ulna/radius Elbow segment flexion-extension and constraint pronation-supination xIMU3-6 Hand Distal limb Wrist/forearm constraint orientation consistency 3.5 Marker-based reference setup The uploaded protocol specifies a dense upper-body marker configuration composed of cluster markers near each instrumented IMU and anatomical markers around the shoulder girdle, elbow, wrist, thorax, pelvis, and neck. Back-view cluster markers are defined for the hand, forearm, upper arm, scapula, thorax, and pelvis, together with anatomical landmarks such as radial and ulnar styloids, medial and lateral humeral epicondyles, acromion angle, C7, and a thoracolumbar landmark [protocol]. Front-view markers include bilateral acromion, clavicular, sternoclavicular, jugular notch, and xiphoid landmarks [protocol]. This layout is well suited to two complementary uses: deriving reference kinematics from marker-based inverse kinematics and quantifying the alignment between inertial and optical segment frames using colocated marker clusters.

Vicon-markers pipeline The Vicon marker pipeline provides the optical-motion reference used to evaluate the IMU/OpenSim estimates. Marker trajectories are exported as .trc files and first used by the OpenSim Scale Tool to generate a subject-specific model. During scaling, distances between experimental marker pairs are compared with corresponding virtual-marker distances on the generic model to compute segment scale factors; the scaled model markers are then adjusted to match the averaged static-pose marker locations. Dynamic trials are processed with OpenSim marker-based inverse kinematics. The spine is represented as a reduced but physiologically constrained chain with lumbar, thoracic, and cervical components, motivated by constant-curvature and augmented full-body approaches that better preserve upper-body realism during complex motion [4,9]. At the shoulder girdle, the scapulothoracic joint is modeled with the Seth ellipsoid-based formulation so that scapular elevation, abduction, and upward rotation can be estimated with greater biomechanical fidelity than would be possible with a simplified ball-joint shoulder alone [3]. At the distal arm, the radius and ulna are modeled as distinct rigid bodies so that elbow flexion-extension and forearm pronation-supination remain separable outcomes. Muscles and unused lower-body joints are removed or locked to reduce computation time, following the logic used in OpenSenseRT [5].

These variables correspond directly to the tables and movement descriptions in your uploaded proposal and protocol materials. 3.8 Calibration procedure A multi-stage calibration procedure is planned before each recording session. First, a static registration trial is acquired with the participant in a neutral pose (REFER TO FIGURE XX). This trial is used by the OpenSense IMUPlacer step to compute a fixed sensor-to-segment offset for each IMU. Let G denote the OpenSim ground frame, B the anatomical body frame carrying IMU i, and F i i the local IMU frame. With the convention that R maps a vector written in XY frame X into frame Y , the first sample of the calibration trial, t , provides the model orientation R (t ) and the measured IMU orientation R (t ). In implementation, IMU columns named (bodyname)_imu are BiG FiBi matched to their corresponding OpenSim bodies, and the resulting R is FiBi stored as a PhysicalOffsetFrame. A separate global alignment step transfers all measurements into a common world-coordinate system by aligning the OpenSim ground frame to the xIMU global frame, using the pelvis sensor as the reference.

In addition to the static alignment step, a functional calibration extension adapted from the OpenIMUs/Di Raimondo approach will be tested with the explicit goal of reducing joint-angle error beyond what can be achieved with a static pose alone [15]. This extension is motivated by recent upper-limb calibration work showing that clinically practical functional or hybrid methods can materially improve performance relative to simple pose-based alignment [7,8]. (MAYBE ADD A TABLE IN APPENDEX FOR ALL THE MOVEMENT DESCRIPTIONS). Each movement is performed seven times and participants are instructed to move at a comfortable slow pace. 3.10 Real-time inverse kinematics pipeline At run time, quaternion orientations from the IMUs are streamed into the inverse-kinematics solver as virtual IMU orientations attached to the model segments.

The OpenSenseRT architecture uses two parallel threads: one for sensor sampling and orientation estimation, and one for inverse kinematics, with the solution at the previous time step used as the initial guess for the next frame [5]. We have modified this to directly read the quaternions from the sensors using UDP communication. The sensors use the Madgwick sensor fusion algorithm to produce quaternion data [REFERENCE]. The inverse-kinematics objective is to minimize the orientation discrepancy between measured and virtual IMUs subject to joint constraints. Using a constrained biomechanical model is especially important here because it helps preserve physiologically plausible trunk and scapular motion, reduces spurious solutions that can occur with unconstrained segment chaining, and yields compensation measures that remain interpretable to clinicians. Consistent with OpenSenseRT, latency will be minimized by simplifying the model, locking unused joints, and carrying forward the previous state estimate [5].

s that remain interpretable to clinicians. Consistent with OpenSenseRT, latency will be minimized by simplifying the model, locking unused joints, and carrying forward the previous state estimate [5]. 3.11 Reference kinematics, synchronization, and preprocessing Reference kinematics will be obtained from marker trajectories using markerbased inverse kinematics in OpenSim, not from proprietary black-box angle outputs alone, so that the inertial and optical pipelines are compared in a common biomechanical framework. This choice is consistent with both OpenSim best practice and recent validation studies in which optical reference trajectories are first mapped to a musculoskeletal model before angle comparison [1,5,8]. For offline analysis, the IMU and marker-based data streams should be synchronized to a common time base, inspected for missing markers or dropout, and resampled when necessary before final comparison. Where shoulder angle definitions are susceptible to gimbal lock, projected-vector definitions should be preferred over naïve Euler decompositions, following the strategy used in recent upper-limb validation work [8]. Because scapular, humeral, and forearm motion are all subject to soft-tissue artifact, segment-specific sensitivity analyses should be retained in the statistical analysis rather than relying on a single pooled error metric. 3.12 Outcome measures The primary validation endpoints are continuous-angle agreement metrics for the five target upper-limb movement variables.

Secondary endpoints quantify the extent to which the system captures compensatory trunk and shouldergirdle motion. From a rehabilitation perspective, these secondary metrics are as important as the primary joint angles because the clinical value of the system depends on its ability to distinguish correct therapeutic movement from compensation-driven task completion. 3.13 Statistical analysis The planned statistical analysis should combine waveform-level, scalar, and compensation-detection metrics. For each kinematic variable, agreement between the IMU/OpenSim pipeline and the marker-based reference should be summarized using root-mean-square error (RMSE), mean absolute error, and Bland-Altman bias and limits of agreement [5,6,8,13]. Range-of-motion agreement should additionally be reported per trial and per participant, because some errors may be acceptable for continuous tracking yet clinically problematic for peak-angle targets. Ablation analyses are recommended for calibration strategy and model structure. The analysis compared a static-only calibration approach with the proposed static-plus-functional calibration protocol. Two shoulder models, Russell and Maier [9,19], were used and compared for the range of motion and compensation detection. Expected results Because this manuscript is being drafted before the final dataset has been processed, the present section is written as a target result structure rather than as completed findings. Based on the current literature, several outcome patterns are plausible and should be explicitly tested. • Primary kinematic validity. Expect the strongest agreement for trunk motion, shoulder flexion/extension, shoulder abduction/adduction, and elbow flexion/extension, with more modest performance for shoulder internal/external rotation and forearm pronation/supination, scapula elevation/depression and shoulder protraction/retraction [6,8,13]. • Calibration benefit. Expect the functional calibration extension to reduce error relative to static-only calibration, particularly for upper arm, forearm, and upper torse segments where sensor-to-segment offsets dominate total error [7,8]. • Model benefit. Compare the available models for running IK and evaluate their suitability for the analysis, and compare them with the plane-based new method to see which provides more accurate results compared to the gold standard marker based IK results[3,9]. • Compensation sensitivity.

Expect compensation metrics derived from trunk motion to be more robust than scapular translations/rotations, because trunk segments are less affected by local soft-tissue artifact than the scapula and forearm [8,11,12]. • Real-time feasibility. Some analysis on the computation rate to show that it is sufficient for feedback during rehabilitation tasks, especially because the model is reduced and limited to the relevant upper-body chain [5]. Figure 10: Enter Caption ## 5. Discussion outline (rough bullet structure) • Why this study matters. Emphasize that home rehabilitation needs movement-quality feedback, not only task completion, and that a validated IMU/OpenSim pipeline can support scalable, repeatable assessment outside the lab.
• Interpret the validity pattern by degree of freedom. Discuss why sagittalplane and trunk measures tend to be easier, whereas shoulder axial rotation and pronation-supination remain challenging because of axis definition, soft-tissue artifact, and calibration sensitivity.
• Calibration as a main error source. Compare your findings with pose-only and hybrid functional calibration papers. Make the point that better calibration may matter as much as better sensors.
• Value of scapula-inclusive modeling. Explain that compensation during shoulder rehabilitation often shifts into the shoulder girdle and trunk; therefore a simplified shoulder model can miss clinically relevant behavior even if gross humeral angles look acceptable.
• Clinical interpretation. Explain how the validated variables map onto therapeutic decisions: whether the patient achieved the requested ROM, whether trunk or shoulder hiking was used, and whether the system can provide corrective feedback in real time.
• Limitations. Healthy-adult validation is necessary but not sufficient; performance may worsen in stroke because of slower, less isolated motion, spasticity, abnormal posture, and difficulty achieving standard calibration

-adult validation is necessary but not sufficient; performance may worsen in stroke because of slower, less isolated motion, spasticity, abnormal posture, and difficulty achieving standard calibration

poses. Scapular measures may also remain less certain than trunk and humeral measures.
• Methodological limitations. Discuss soft-tissue artifact, marker-based reference limitations, differences between OpenSim and ISB angle conventions, sensor placement variability, and the challenge of choosing compensation thresholds, the reasons for the existing error and the signal discrepancy and comparing our results with the other methods in literature and justifying why despite our error we can still use the final method moving forward.
• Next step. Position this validation study as the enabling layer for the ongoing mixed-reality home-rehabilitation project rather than the final clinical endpoint.
6. Conclusion This draft manuscript frames a rigorous validation study for real-time IMUbased upper-body tracking in shoulder rehabilitation. By combining a compact wearable sensor set, a scapula-inclusive OpenSim model, a protocol-based marker reference, and explicit compensation metrics, the study is positioned to answer the core translational question for your broader platform: whether a practical wearable system can quantify not only target movement but also clinically meaningful compensations with accuracy sufficient for real-time home feedback. If the expected validity patterns are achieved, the study will justify progression to the next phase of the project, namely deployment of the validated kinematic engine within the ongoing mixed-reality, home-based post-stroke rehabilitation system.
